completion_definition: A behavior spec node is complete IFF (a) BDD scenarios with positive and falsification witnesses exist and pass for every behavior listed in completes_behaviors on every interface declared by that behavior, and (b) the subjective playbook has been walked end-to-end with a human accepting each step on every declared interface. Foundation spec nodes are complete IFF the definition_of_done in the foundation spec passes; they intentionally complete zero behaviors.

milestones.title: Account, Identity & Sign-in Foundation
milestones.goal: A person can create an account, sign into it, organize work under organizations they create or are invited to, and switch between identities cleanly across interfaces.
milestones.summary: Sign-up, sign-in, and the multi-account / multi-organization identity model that every other behavior hangs off. Self-signup, invitation-based account creation, organization creation, two-sided invitation flow (invite + join with existing or new account), membership departure (leave/remove with last-admin invariant), and active-account / active-organization context that scopes every other behavior. Boundary: granular permissions and role templates live in M-0004; project context lives in M-0003; configuration tied to identity lives in M-0002. Org deletion (B-0067) lives in M-0004 because it depends on project-disposition policy.

milestones.title: Configuration & Secret Storage
milestones.goal: Users can manage configuration at user / account / project / organization tiers and store secrets safely without exposing values, with rotation, revocation, and visibility primitives in place.
milestones.summary: Configuration tiers (user / account / project / organization) and credential / secret storage with rotation, revocation, and visibility primitives. Wave A (no project dep) covers user-tier configuration + credentials, organization-tier secrets, notification preferences, cross-device sync, see-usage / rotate-revoke / detect-issues primitives. Wave B (after M-0003 brings projects online) covers project-tier configuration (methodology, runtime defaults, verification gates), project-scoped secrets, project config history, account-shared and cross-project shared defaults, and the cross-tier 'distinguish personal vs shared' visibility rule. Boundary: granular access policy on credentials lives in M-0004; configuration driving runtime placement lives in M-0010; integrations connecting external providers live in M-0009.

milestones.title: Project Bootstrap & Asset Install
milestones.goal: A user can connect a repo or create a new project, install Tanren methodology assets (commands, standards, agent integrations), detect drift, upgrade, and uninstall without losing user work.
milestones.summary: Three sub-clusters: (1) first-run setup chain (deployment posture → identity + SCM provider → first-run checklist umbrella → ready first project); (2) project lifecycle (connect or create a project, see+switch projects, disconnect, join an existing project with current context); (3) CLI-only install/asset management (bootstrap with selectable agent integrations, drift detection, runtime use of installed standards, upgrade with preview, uninstall preserving user work). The CLI install path is orthogonal to account-level project registration — install is filesystem, registration is account-level. Boundary: provider lifecycle proper is M-0009; project access management is M-0004.

milestones.title: Permissions & Governance
milestones.goal: Permissions, role templates, approval policy, and access management work across project and organization scopes, including service accounts and API keys.
milestones.summary: Five sub-clusters: (1) access visibility and management at project + organization scope (with see vs manage as separate behaviors); (2) self-introspection (see my own permissions); (3) role templates (grant-time bundles, not authorization identities); (4) policy — approval requirements, credential-use policy, test-before-apply, denial explanation; (5) machine identities — service accounts, API keys (create / scope / rotate / revoke / see usage). Plus organization deletion with the project-disposition flow (B-0067 + B-0272). Boundary: identity / org / membership lifecycle is M-0001; configuration tier framework is M-0002; runtime placement decisions are M-0010.

milestones.title: Product Planning Method
milestones.goal: Users can run the plan-product / identify-behaviors / architect-system / craft-roadmap loop, propose and review planning changes, and maintain the accepted behavior catalog.
milestones.summary: The planning loop in product terms. Six sub-clusters: (1) product discovery — turn a vague idea into a brief, define target users / problems / non-goals / constraints / success signals, import existing context; (2) product mission and roadmap as planning context, plus 'keep work aligned with mission' guard; (3) accepted behavior catalog — maintenance, coverage view, coherence review; (4) system architecture record — maintenance and tradeoff review; (5) decision memory — record decisions / preserve rejected alternatives / reuse preferences / surface stale assumptions; (6) planning change governance — propose, review, resolve conflicting product direction. Boundary: implementation assessment is M-0006; spec shaping is M-0007; quality gates on specs are M-0008; recovery operations on past decisions are M-0026.

milestones.title: Implementation Assessment
milestones.goal: Users can assess what implementation appears to satisfy each accepted behavior and review the assessment's uncertainty.
milestones.summary: The assess-implementation flow: classify each accepted behavior against current implementation as missing / implemented / asserted / stale / regressed / uncertain, and surface the uncertainty for review. Two behaviors only because the assessment SUBSYSTEM owns the broader 'route findings into planning proposals' work elsewhere (M-0027). Boundary: the verification-status field on a behavior is owned by M-0005's behavior-catalog maintenance node; this milestone is the analytical pass that recommends status changes.

milestones.title: Spec Shaping & Lifecycle
milestones.goal: Specs can be drafted, shaped, marked ready, blocked, archived, grouped into milestones and initiatives, and have acceptance criteria and dependencies declared.
milestones.summary: Spec as the unit of work. Three sub-clusters: (1) spec creation (manual draft, prefilled from external ticket, generated from roadmap items); (2) spec shaping for prioritization (acceptance criteria, dependencies including cross-project, ready-state shaping); (3) spec lifecycle states (Draft / Backlog / Ready / Blocked / Archived plus see-state, mark-ready, move-to-backlog, archive); plus grouping (milestone, initiative) and bulk actions. Boundary: spec readiness gates (clarifying questions, edge cases, oversize, untestable) are M-0008; the running-loop side of lifecycle (Running / Awaiting walk / Done) is M-0011 + M-0014.

milestones.title: Spec Readiness & Quality Gates
milestones.goal: Specs are vetted before going to implementation: clarifying questions surface, edge cases are identified, oversized specs are split, untestable specs are blocked.
milestones.summary: Pre-implementation quality gates on specs. Five concerns: clarifying questions for vague work, missing-edge-case identification, oversize detection with split proposals, untestable-behavior readiness blocks, and 'why is this spec not ready' explanations. Boundary: post-implementation quality (gate / audit / adherence / demo) is M-0013; behavior proof itself is M-0012; spec lifecycle states are M-0007.

milestones.title: Provider Integrations — Source Control & CI
milestones.goal: Users can connect source-control and CI providers, see provider health and reachable resources, recover from auth failures, and audit external actions.
milestones.summary: Outbound provider connections for source-control + CI. Connection records, capability discovery, authorization recovery, normalized provider signals, action lifecycle (request / dispatch / succeed / fail / reconcile), audit of external actions. Source-control integration also owns PR/branch creation, merge handoff, CI status ingestion. Boundary: external-tracker integration (Linear/Jira issues) is M-0015; webhook delivery for outbound integration clients is M-0017; cloud / VM / infrastructure providers are M-0010 + M-0020; first-run provider connect flow shipped in M-0003 R-0016.

milestones.title: Runtime & Worker Contracts
milestones.goal: Tanren can run isolated work in execution targets, manage harness credentials and access, surface harness-neutral failures, retry transient failures, and honor worker contracts (assignment, progress, blockers, cancellation).
milestones.summary: Two parallel concerns. (1) Runtime substrate from the user's perspective: harness credentials, project harness selection, harness readiness, isolated execution start, see-where-running, see-environment-access, stop/recover, harness-neutral runtime failures, transient retry, VM/remote target management, retire/drain target, worker-scoped temporary access, cloud VM provider account management. (2) Runtime-actor contracts (the agent-worker protocol Tanren itself enforces): receive scoped assignment, refuse invalid, report progress / submit results / report blockers / report terminal outcome, honor cancellation, avoid duplicate visible work, use only granted access, preserve output without leaking secrets, distinguish failure classes, reconcile interrupted sessions. Boundary: implementation-loop user controls (start / pause / resume) are M-0011; operational health views are M-0020.

milestones.title: Implementation Loop
milestones.goal: Users can start, observe, pause, resume, cancel, and respond to blockers in implementation loops, including auto-start on serial execution and coordinated candidate implementations.
milestones.summary: User-facing loop control across implementation work. Manual start, auto-start on serial execution policy, see loop state, notification on completion, blocker response, walk trigger after completion, pause / resume, inspect live activity, dependency-blocking, cancel, coordinate candidate implementations for one spec. Boundary: runtime substrate (where loops run) is M-0010; quality gates that decide loop progress are M-0013; walk and merge that follow loop completion are M-0014.

milestones.title: Behavior Proof Harness
milestones.goal: BDD-driven behavior proof produces positive and falsification witnesses for accepted behaviors and supports moving verification status to asserted.
milestones.summary: The proof framework that all later quality work depends on. Owns: linking BDD feature files to behavior IDs, producing positive + falsification witnesses, interpreting coverage and mutation testing as gap signals, supplying proof-linked assertion state for assessment. F-0001 ships the harness machinery; M-0012 ships the proof METHODOLOGY (witness rules, assertion-status criteria, freshness model). One behavior owns it (B-0285) but this milestone is load-bearing for M-0013, M-0014, and the entire 'completion' rule of every other spec node.

milestones.title: Quality Gates, Audit & Adherence
milestones.goal: Quality controls (gates, audit, adherence, demo, findings lifecycle) decide whether work progresses past task and candidate validation.
milestones.summary: The post-implementation quality stack: phase taxonomy (mode / intent / scope / autonomy), baseline controls (task gate / audit / adherence; candidate gate / audit / adherence / demo), guard semantics with staleness, finding lifecycle (severity, defer disposition, routing). Owns finding-tracking that blocks readiness (B-0080) and the codebase-wide audit (B-0096). Boundary: spec-readiness gates (pre-implementation) are M-0008; behavior proof itself is M-0012; observation / dashboards over findings are M-0019.

milestones.title: Walk, Review & Merge
milestones.goal: Walked work can be reviewed, accepted or rejected with follow-up routing, turned into pull requests, merged, and have completed execution resources cleaned up.
milestones.summary: End-of-loop human handoff. Three sub-clusters: (1) walk acceptance — present demo summary / show expected vs actual / show changed surfaces and residual risks / preserve walk acceptance record; (2) review-merge — review delivered behavior during walk, accept or reject with follow-up routing, create PR, see PR / CI state, route review feedback, mark feedback addressed-or-out-of-scope, merge accepted work, clean up completed execution resources; (3) recovery — recover from merge conflicts after parallel work lands, detect intent conflicts after related work lands. Boundary: PR mechanics rely on M-0009 source-control integration; behavior proof underlies all walk decisions (M-0012).

milestones.title: External Tracker Integration
milestones.goal: External issue trackers can be connected for outbound issues and ticket-prefilled spec creation, with disconnect, replace, and outbound review supported.
milestones.summary: Bidirectional sync with Linear / Jira / GitHub Issues, but Tanren-canonical. Connect external tracker capabilities, see unshaped tickets, see outbound issues Tanren has pushed, configure outbound issue review mode, see external reference state from a spec, quick-access external links from a spec, disconnect or replace tracker, review queued outbound issues. Boundary: spec creation prefilled from a ticket (B-0075) is M-0007; integration client contracts (the Tanren API surface itself) are M-0016.

milestones.title: Integration Client Contracts
milestones.goal: External clients can call Tanren via API / MCP with retry-safe requests, machine-readable validation errors, schema versioning, attribution, rate-limit signaling, replay safety, and read-model observation.
milestones.summary: The contract surface for external automation talking to Tanren via the API + MCP interfaces (machine-only). Retry-safe (idempotent) create/update, machine-readable validation errors, schema-version negotiation, attribution of external automation, deny-with-permission-boundary, rate-limit and backpressure reporting, observe state through read models / event streams, safely replay client requests after failure. Boundary: webhook delivery is M-0017; cross-interface continuity (web / cli / tui consistency) is M-0021; provider integrations going OUT to external systems are M-0009 + M-0015.

milestones.title: Webhooks & Event Streaming
milestones.goal: Webhook endpoints can be configured and managed, deliveries are reliable and dedupe-able, and delivery failures can be handled.
milestones.summary: Outbound webhook surface for external clients to receive Tanren events. Manage webhook endpoints, deliver webhooks reliably so consumers can process events without ambiguity (with retry, ordering, duplicate-detection metadata), handle webhook delivery failures. Boundary: integration client contracts (synchronous request/response) are M-0016; provider integrations consuming events are M-0009.

milestones.title: Team Coordination
milestones.goal: Teammates can see each other's work, assist or take over loops, transfer ownership, leave handoff notes, claim or assign work, and avoid duplicate work.
milestones.summary: Multi-user collaboration on shared project work. See teammates' active work, assist a teammate's loop temporarily, transfer loop ownership, configure assist/takeover rules, prevent uncoordinated concurrent loops, see loop action history, request takeover, see loops under a milestone or initiative, see work needing my attention, claim ownership, assign to another builder, leave handoff notes, route blockers to responders, detect overlapping work, onboard a builder to shared project, see team attention load (without performance surveillance). Boundary: granular permissions on assist/takeover are M-0004; cross-project visibility is M-0019.

milestones.title: Observation, Dashboards & Reports
milestones.goal: Users can see project overviews, pipelines, quality and health signals, blocked work, forecasts, post-release outcomes, and provenance behind summaries.
milestones.summary: The read-side composition layer over canonical state. Built incrementally — each sub-node depends on its specific upstream data sources. Includes work pipeline (velocity / throughput), quality / health / risk signals, time-window comparison, per-builder breakdown, scope selection, what-changed-since-last-visit, project overview, roadmap progress against goals, blocked work overview, delivery forecast and risk, quality / risk trends, what-supports-a-summary (provenance), recently shipped outcomes, post-release health, open decisions, work drift from mission/standards, cross-project dependency risk, exported read-only reports, observer digest subscription, planned vs actual delivery comparison, what-changed-since-last-report. Boundary: canonical event log is owned by every other milestone that emits events; dashboards just compose them.

milestones.title: Operations & Health
milestones.goal: Operators can see and manage execution-infrastructure health, pause and resume work, run codebase audits and DR validation, export audit logs and operational data, track cost and quota.
milestones.summary: Operator-tier maintenance. Export account or project data for backup / migration, restore from backup, see operational health of execution infrastructure, pause new work for a project / organization, resume new work, audit placement and approval decisions, track cost and quota usage across providers, enter maintenance or incident mode, run disaster recovery validation, export operational audit logs. Boundary: runtime substrate (where work runs) is M-0010; observation / dashboards / reports are M-0019; finding-routing into planning is M-0027.

milestones.title: Cross-Interface Continuity & Notifications
milestones.goal: State and validation errors are coherent across interfaces, work can continue across interfaces, and notifications reach the right user.
milestones.summary: Surface-parity guarantees and the notification delivery side of preferences (the preference configuration is in M-0002 R-0010). Coherent state across interfaces (web / api / mcp / cli / tui), continue same work from another interface, consistent validation errors across interfaces, deliver notification when a loop completes or needs attention. Boundary: notification preferences are M-0002 R-0010; interface contract specifics (idempotency, errors) are M-0016.

milestones.title: Repo Understanding & Standards Evolution
milestones.goal: Tanren can analyze imported repositories, detect commands, summarize architecture and risk, propose initial configuration, and evolve project standards with conflict detection.
milestones.summary: First-pass repo intelligence + standards evolution loop. Analyze imported repository, detect build/test/lint/release commands, summarize architecture and major risks, propose initial project configuration, discover project standards from source signals, propose standards updates from repeated findings, explain which standards influenced a decision, detect conflicting standards. Boundary: provider connection itself is M-0009; standards as configuration tier is M-0002; standards-as-policy enforcement is M-0013.

milestones.title: Release & Learning Loop
milestones.goal: Users can prepare release notes, verify post-release state, ingest post-ship feedback, link shipped changes to roadmap, and update planning from outcomes.
milestones.summary: Closing the loop after merge. Prepare release notes from accepted work, verify post-release state, link shipped changes back to roadmap and specs, ingest post-ship feedback, update roadmap and specs from post-ship outcomes, configure what counts as 'shipped' for a project. Boundary: merge itself is M-0014; observation of post-release health is M-0019; feedback intake mechanics are M-0027.

milestones.title: Autonomy Controls
milestones.goal: Users can configure autonomy level, require approval for sensitive actions, let low-risk work continue until a blocker, and stop automation at user-set boundaries.
milestones.summary: How much Tanren is allowed to do without asking. Configure autonomy level, require approval before sensitive actions, let low-risk work continue until a blocker, stop automation at user-set boundaries, see gated-action approval state, collect required approvals. Boundary: organization approval policy is M-0004 R-0043; granular permissions are M-0004; what counts as 'sensitive' is policy + this milestone's user-tunable knobs.

milestones.title: Prioritization & Replanning
milestones.goal: Users can compare candidate work, recommend roadmap sequencing with tradeoffs, defer work with rationale, and rebalance the roadmap after new source signals.
milestones.summary: Roadmap maintenance after the initial craft. Compare candidate work, recommend roadmap sequencing with tradeoffs, defer work with explicit rationale, rebalance the roadmap after new source signals. Boundary: initial roadmap creation is M-0005's craft-roadmap node; spec-level prioritization tooling is M-0007.

milestones.title: Decision Memory, Undo & Recovery
milestones.goal: Decisions and rejected alternatives are durable, prior user preferences are reused when shaping work, stale assumptions are surfaced, and prior revisions can be restored or work reverted without losing history.
milestones.summary: The recovery / undo side of the planning method. Restore a previous revision without deleting history, revert landed work through controlled follow-up, recover from a bad planning decision without losing history. Decision MEMORY itself (record decisions / preserve rejected alternatives / reuse preferences / surface stale assumptions) is owned by M-0005; this milestone is the recovery operations on top of that memory.

milestones.title: Proactive Analysis & Findings Routing
milestones.goal: Customer feedback, audit findings, bug reports, and proactive analyses (mutation, security, benchmarks) can be captured and routed into planning.
milestones.summary: The intake side that feeds product planning. Capture human-authored product signals (customer feedback, meeting notes — bundled in B-0094), turn audit findings into specs or backlog items, classify bug reports against behavior status, route proactive analysis into planning, schedule or run proactive analysis, review proactive analysis recommendations. Boundary: spec-creation intake (manual / from ticket / from roadmap) is M-0007; post-ship feedback intake is M-0023.

nodes.title: Foundation Spec — Minimum Buildable Tanren
nodes.scope:
One-time bootstrap from the scaffolding-only state to a 'minimum buildable Tanren' where every subsystem has its bones in place and every public interface ID (web, api, mcp, cli, tui) has runnable scaffolding. After this lands, every subsequent roadmap spec is a thin end-to-end behavior slice that adds its surface on every interface — there is no future spec gated on 'an interface doesn't exist yet'.

Shipped: workspace member crates with skeletal lib.rs/main.rs — four foundation crates (domain, contract, policy, observability), three capability crates (store, app-services, scheduler), eleven subsystem stub crates (planner, orchestrator, identity-policy, configuration-secrets, provider-integrations, client-integrations, behavior-proof, assessment, observation, quality-controls, testkit), two adapter contract umbrellas (runtime, harness — contract-only; concrete adapter crates land with the R-* slice that needs each one), and the BDD step-definition home (bdd). Five backend binaries (tanrend, tanren-api, tanren-cli, tanren-mcp, tanren-tui) each running a minimal scaffold (api: a `/health` endpoint plus `/openapi.json`; cli: `--version`, `health`, and `migrate up`; mcp: an empty `rmcp` tool registry over stdio transport; tui: an empty Ratatui shell). Web frontend skeleton at apps/web/ (Next.js 16 + Turbopack + pnpm catalog + ts-go typecheck + oxlint + prettier) renders a single placeholder page that fetches `/health` from the api. xtask binary with `check-rust-test-surface` and `check-suppression` subcommands, plus the existing inline `just check-lines`/`check-deps` recipes. Postgres + sea-orm-migration infrastructure with one no-op migration creating the canonical `events` table; runner is exposed via `tanren-cli migrate up`. Event log append/read primitives on the events table. BDD harness (cucumber-rs) wired into `just tests`, with the harness machinery exercised by Rust unit tests inside `tanren-bdd` (the only crate in the workspace permitted to define `#[test]` items, enforced by `xtask check-rust-test-surface`). ZERO feature files — `just tests` exits 0 with 'no scenarios run, harness functional'. Mutation pipeline wired via `just mutation` (nightly only, not part of `just ci`). Full `just ci` exits 0 with every aggregator stage active and no commented TODOs.

Deferred to R-* slices: per-vendor runtime substrate adapters (`tanren-runtime-docker`, `tanren-runtime-remote`, plus future `tanren-runtime-{hetzner,gcp,aws,...}`) and per-vendor harness adapters (`tanren-harness-claude`, `tanren-harness-codex`, `tanren-harness-opencode`). The runtime architecture explicitly rejects unmanaged-local-worktree execution as a core path, so no `tanren-runtime-local` crate exists. The `install-commands-check` xtask gate and the agent integration tree renderer (.claude/commands, .codex/skills, .opencode/commands) are also deferred to a later slice; the `commands/project/` source-of-truth (4 command files) ships unchanged. utoipa-derive-based OpenAPI generation enters with R-0001 once there are real request/response types worth deriving from — F-0001 serves a hand-written `/openapi.json` stub.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: F-0001 establishes scaffolding and infrastructure ONLY. It ships zero feature files and completes zero behaviors. The first feature file in tests/bdd/features/ is shipped by R-0001 along with the first real behavior assertion. F-0001 ensures the web/api/cli/mcp/tui surfaces exist and can host behavior endpoints; everything after F-0001 is a thin behavior slice that adds its endpoint/command/tool/screen/page on each surface.

expected_evidence.description: `cargo build --workspace --locked` succeeds; every workspace member compiles. The web frontend builds via its own toolchain wired into `just ci`.

`just ci` exits 0 with all aggregator stages re-enabled (deps locked, format, workflow lint, docs, line budget, suppression guard, dependency boundaries, rust test surface, cargo check, clippy, tests, deny, doc, machete, install drift).

Cucumber-rs is in workspace.dependencies; the bdd crate exposes a step-definition registry that compiles and is exercised by a Rust unit test; `just tests` runs the harness against tests/bdd/features/ and exits 0 with zero scenarios run. NO placeholder or 'fake-behavior' feature files are shipped — feature files only enter the repo with R-0001 onwards.

`just mutation` runs cargo-mutants against the bdd crate's step-definition machinery and produces a report. With no real scenarios yet, the report is necessarily empty; the test is that the mutation pipeline runs without error.

playbook: Run `just install`; verify the four backend binaries are produced and the web frontend builds.
Start the api binary; curl `/health`; verify 200 with a structured payload (no behavior assertion — just liveness).
Run `tanren-cli --version`; verify it prints and exits 0.
Connect to tanren-mcp from the Claude Code CLI as the experimental MCP client; verify the server responds to a list-tools request (the response can be empty — Tanren has no tools yet).
Run `tanren-tui`; verify the empty shell renders and exits cleanly.
Open the web frontend; verify the placeholder page loads against the api.
Run a Postgres migration via the migration runner; verify it completes against an empty database.
Run `just tests`; verify the cucumber harness reports 'no scenarios' and exits 0.
Run `just mutation` once outside `just ci` to confirm cargo-mutants is wired and produces a (necessarily empty) report; mutation is a nightly job, not a CI gate.
Confirm `just ci` is fully green and NO aggregator stage is commented out as a TODO. `just mutation` is intentionally NOT part of `just ci`.
nodes.rationale: Foundation is a one-time exception to the 'every spec completes a behavior' rule. The methodological reason is twofold: (a) a strict no-exceptions reading would force R-0001 to bootstrap the kernel + every interface + the BDD harness AND prove B-0043 — making R-0001 unshapeable; and (b) the BDD harness itself cannot be exercised by 'fake-behavior' feature files without violating the tests/bdd/README rule that every active scenario must reference a real B-XXXX. By isolating bootstrap into F-0001 we let every R-XXXX spec be a genuine thin behavior slice that fully completes its declared behavior on every declared interface, and the harness is exercised legitimately starting from R-0001's first real feature file.
definition_of_done: All workspace lints (workspace.lints.rust, workspace.lints.clippy) apply to every member crate
Five backend binaries (tanrend, tanren-api, tanren-cli, tanren-mcp, tanren-tui) produced and runnable
Web frontend skeleton builds via pnpm + Turbopack and renders a placeholder page consuming the api
Postgres connection + migrations runner functional, exposed via `tanren-cli migrate up` (one example no-op migration ships)
Cucumber-rs is in workspace dependencies; bdd crate's step-definition machinery is exercised by Rust unit tests; `just tests` runs the harness with zero feature files and exits 0

Mutation pipeline wires `just mutation` to cargo-mutants (recipe runs and exits 0 against zero scenarios); the recipe is reserved for a nightly main-branch job that uploads failure artifacts and is intentionally NOT invoked by `just ci`
`just ci` exits 0 with NO aggregator stages commented out — every TODO from the rewrite reset is resolved
risks: Foundation is large by necessity. Estimate 3-5 weeks for a small team (web frontend stack adds to the original kernel-only estimate). We accept this as a one-time cost.

F-0001 must produce auth primitives (session model, credential verification trait) without committing to a specific mechanism — the architecture commits to local + OIDC (technology.md), but mechanism wiring is deferred to R-0001 because Foundation has no user identity. Care is needed to avoid baking in shape that R-0001 must then unwind.

Workspace lint policy (deny unwrap/panic/todo/print, forbid unsafe, deny allow_attributes) is the most likely cause of mid-Foundation churn — every stub crate must compile clean under the strict ruleset on first commit. Plan for time to reshape early stubs that fall foul of clippy::pedantic.

nodes.title: Foundation Correction — HTTP MCP, BDD convention, validators
nodes.scope: One-time correction of four F-0001 misalignments with the accepted architecture under docs/architecture/, applied before any R-* node lands. (1) Replaces the empty rmcp stdio entry point in bin/tanren-mcp with an axum-hosted Streamable HTTP service gated behind an API-key middleware that emits the shared auth_required / permission_denied error taxonomy from interfaces.md. The bootstrap key reads from the TANREN_MCP_API_KEY environment variable; the real credential store lands with R-0008 (B-0048 / B-0125). (2) Adds the xtask check-bdd-tags validator: walks tests/bdd/features for filename to feature-tag match, the closed tag allowlist, strict-equality interface coverage against docs/behaviors and docs/roadmap/dag.json, and the forbidden Gherkin-construct list. Wired into just check. (3) Locks the BDD .feature convention in docs/architecture/subsystems/behavior-proof.md (BDD Tagging And File Convention): one .feature per behavior, feature-level B-XXXX tag, scenario-level positive or falsification plus 1 to 2 interface tags, no Scenario Outline or Examples, and per-interface positive plus falsification coverage when the DAG node lists falsification witnesses. (4) Tightens scripts/roadmap_check.py with a feature-file inventory check (every B-XXXX-*.feature maps to an accepted behavior with a DAG node) and tightens just check-deps with a positive assertion that tanren-mcp depends on axum (mechanical guard against accidental reversion to stdio). Updates the foundation_spec_id pointer from F-0001 to F-0002 so every R-* behavior node transitively depends on the corrected foundation.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: F-0001 owned the initial scaffolding (workspace, crates, five interface scaffolds, BDD harness, postgres + migrations). F-0002 corrects four specific architectural misalignments left in F-0001 (stdio MCP, missing tag enforcement, missing BDD convention, dependency-shape drift). All R-* behavior nodes — starting with R-0001 — depend on F-0002 (which transitively pulls in F-0001) so they inherit the corrected foundation. The real MCP credential store, including organisation- and project-scoped service-account keys, is owned by R-0008 (B-0048 / B-0125); F-0002 ships the enforcement point with a single env-sourced bootstrap key as a placeholder.

expected_evidence.description: cargo build --workspace --locked succeeds; tanren-mcp links axum, rmcp (transport-streamable-http-server), tower, tower-http; cargo tree -p tanren-mcp shows no stdio transport feature on rmcp.
just ci exits 0 with all aggregator stages including the new bdd tags step (xtask check-bdd-tags) and the tightened dependency boundaries step (axum-required for tanren-mcp).

Start tanren-mcp with TANREN_MCP_API_KEY set; curl /health returns 200 with the tanren-app-services HealthReport shape; curl /mcp without Authorization returns 401 with auth_required; curl /mcp with a wrong key returns 403 with permission_denied; curl /mcp with the correct key returns the Streamable HTTP MCP greeting and tools/list resolves to an empty array.

xtask check-bdd-tags runs against an empty tests/bdd/features tree and exits 0 with the vacuous-pass message; runs against a malformed B-XXXX-*.feature fixture and hard-fails with precise per-violation messages; runs against a partial-coverage fixture and hard-fails listing the uncovered interfaces (no soft-coverage lane).
python3 scripts/roadmap_check.py exits 0 against the post-merge DAG; foundation_spec_id is F-0002; every R-* node transitively reaches F-0002.

playbook: Migrate bin/tanren-mcp from rmcp stdio to rmcp Streamable HTTP server hosted under axum, mirroring tanren-api posture.
Add an axum middleware that requires Authorization: Bearer <key> (or X-API-Key) on /mcp and rejects with auth_required / permission_denied per interfaces.md error taxonomy.
Source the bootstrap key from TANREN_MCP_API_KEY; emit a startup warning when unset.
Drop rmcp transport-io feature in workspace deps; enable transport-streamable-http-server.
Add tokio-util to workspace deps for rmcp CancellationToken.
Add the check-bdd-tags xtask subcommand with a closed tag allowlist, strict-equality interface coverage, behavior-catalog cross-check, and DAG-evidence cross-check.
Wire check-bdd-tags into just check (after check-rust-test-surface).
Tighten just check-deps with a positive assertion that tanren-mcp depends on axum.
Extend scripts/roadmap_check.py with a tests/bdd/features inventory check (every B-XXXX-*.feature maps to accepted behavior + DAG evidence).
Document the BDD convention in docs/architecture/subsystems/behavior-proof.md (new BDD Tagging And File Convention section).
Rewrite tests/bdd/README.md to point at the canonical convention and the validator commands.
Add a one-line BDD-convention pointer to CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md.

Add F-0002 to docs/roadmap/dag.json; switch foundation_spec_id from F-0001 to F-0002; repoint R-0001 and R-0023 from F-0001 to F-0002 (other R-* nodes already reach F-0002 transitively through their R-* parents).
Run python3 scripts/roadmap_check.py and just ci; both must exit 0.
Manually verify the MCP HTTP service end-to-end: spin up postgres, run tanren-cli migrate up, start tanren-mcp with a bootstrap key, exercise /health and /mcp with curl.
Manually exercise check-bdd-tags with malformed and partial-coverage fixtures; confirm the failures are precise and the green path stays green.
nodes.rationale: F-0001 set up scaffolding for every subsystem and interface but baked four small mismatches against the architecture record — most visibly an MCP service running over stdio when docs/architecture/subsystems/interfaces.md and docs/architecture/technology.md (rejected-alternatives section) mandate Streamable HTTP. Three R-0001 attempts on top of F-0001 each invented their own BDD convention because tests/bdd/README declared the contract but no validator enforced it. F-0002 closes those gaps before any behavior slice lands so the first wave of R-* nodes (R-0001 through R-0010) inherits a corrected, mechanically-enforced foundation. Foundation no-completes-behaviors carve-out remains: F-0002 produces tooling and corrected scaffolding, not behavior assertion.
definition_of_done: tanren-mcp serves MCP Streamable HTTP under axum on the configured port; cargo tree -p tanren-mcp contains axum and excludes rmcp stdio transport feature.
Auth middleware: /mcp without a credential returns 401 auth_required; with a wrong credential returns 403 permission_denied; with the bootstrap key returns the empty Streamable HTTP MCP surface.
xtask check-bdd-tags exists and is wired into just check (visible in just ci).
scripts/roadmap_check.py enforces tests/bdd/features to DAG to behavior alignment; running it post-merge exits 0 with foundation_spec_id == F-0002.
just check-deps requires axum in tanren-mcp direct deps and continues to reject domain/policy/store from any transport binary.
docs/architecture/subsystems/behavior-proof.md contains the BDD Tagging And File Convention section.
tests/bdd/README.md is rewritten to point at the convention and the validator commands; CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md carry a one-line pointer.
docs/roadmap/dag.json contains F-0002, foundation_spec_id is F-0002, and R-0001 + R-0023 depend on F-0002 (not F-0001).
just ci exits 0; python3 scripts/roadmap_check.py exits 0.
risks: rmcp Streamable HTTP server transport is a network surface — binding to 0.0.0.0 by default makes the service reachable from any container on the host network. Mitigation: the axum middleware refuses every request without an exact bootstrap-key match (constant-time compare); the bind address is overridable via TANREN_MCP_BIND for operators that prefer 127.0.0.1.

The bootstrap-key auth is a placeholder, not the real credential store. Mitigation: the placeholder shape is deliberately narrow (one env var, single-tenant) so R-0008 can swap it for the per-actor service-account model without re-shaping the middleware contract.

F-0002 closes the multi-behavior-feature exception in behavior-proof.md (one .feature proves exactly one behavior). Future authors might reach for the closed exception when they encounter genuinely-coupled behavior pairs (e.g., R-0007 owns B-0059 + B-0060). Mitigation: the convention requires multi-behavior R-* nodes to ship one feature file per completed behavior — the bundling lives at the node level, not the feature level.

Per-interface falsification coverage is stricter than behavior-proof.md where meaningful framing. Some R-* nodes will need to write five falsification scenarios per behavior. Mitigation: the validator emits precise per-interface error messages so authors know exactly which falsification scenarios are missing; the convention section in behavior-proof.md flags the elevation explicitly.

nodes.title: Create an account and sign in
nodes.scope: Add the account-create + sign-in surface on every interface declared by B-0043 (web, api, mcp, cli, tui). Cover both creation pathways (self-signup and invitation-based-creation), the multi-account-per-person rule, the personal-orphans-no-org rule, and the sign-in flow that produces an authenticated session. Invitation-based-creation scenarios use a fixtured invitation record (the user-facing invite-creation flow is R-0005). Foundation has already established the bones of each interface, the API server, the auth primitives, the event log, and the BDD harness — this spec adds account events + projections, the create/sign-in endpoints/pages/commands/tools/screens, and the BDD scenarios that prove all observable outcomes on every interface.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0003 owns switching between multiple accounts. R-0002 owns organization creation. R-0005 owns the invite-sending flow. R-0006 owns invitation acceptance for an EXISTING account. F-0001 already established the kernel + interface scaffolding; this node only adds the account-specific behavior on top.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: self-signup succeeds and the resulting account can sign in. Positive: invitation-based creation joins the inviting org. Positive: same person can hold multiple accounts (independent identifiers). Positive: self-signed-up account belongs to no organization. Falsification: signup with duplicate identifier fails. Falsification: sign-in with wrong credentials fails. Falsification: invitation-based creation with invalid/expired invitation fails. R-0001 ships the FIRST feature file in tests/bdd/features/, exercising the BDD harness scaffolding put in place by F-0001.

playbook: API surface: self-signup via POST; assert session and account record. Sign out, sign back in. Create second account with distinct identifier; assert coexistence. Fixture invitation; accept via API; assert new account joined inviting org.

Web surface: navigate to the sign-up page; complete self-signup; verify the authenticated session. Sign out and sign back in. Navigate to the invitation-acceptance page from a fixtured invitation link; accept; verify membership.

CLI surface: `tanren-cli account create` for a fresh identifier; assert success and session persistence. Repeat for second account. Run `tanren-cli account create --invitation <token>` against fixtured invitation; assert membership.
MCP surface: invoke the create-account tool from an MCP client with fresh identifier; verify return shape and that the account is queryable. Repeat for invitation-based path.
TUI surface: open the sign-up screen; create account; verify session. Open invitation-acceptance screen; accept fixtured invitation; verify membership.
Cross-interface verification: create account via API; sign in via web frontend with same credentials; verify the same identity. Confirm no interface-specific state diverges.

Negative walks per interface: duplicate identifier on each surface; wrong credentials on each surface; expired invitation on each surface. Every failure surfaces an explicit, user-readable reason on the interface where the action was attempted.
nodes.rationale: B-0043 names five interfaces. By the strict completion rule the user has insisted on, the spec must demonstrate the behavior on all five. Foundation makes this tractable by pre-establishing the binaries, web frontend skeleton, and routing scaffolding for each. Bundling self-signup and invitation-creation pathways inside the same spec is necessary because B-0043 names both as observable outcomes; splitting would leave the behavior incomplete.
risks: Authentication mechanism choice (password / SSO / passwordless) is explicitly out of scope of B-0043 but must be picked here. Likely passwordless or simple password to start; can be revisited later. The BDD scenarios must be mechanism-neutral.
Web sign-up + sign-in is the highest-design-effort surface (real UX flow with form validation, error states, success redirect). Expect web scope to dominate the spec.
TUI account-create is the most novel UX surface for the team. Expect to spend disproportionate review time on it.

nodes.title: Create an organization
nodes.scope: Establish the organization entity, the organization-creation flow, and the bootstrap administrative permissions held by the creator (invite, manage access, configure, set policy, delete). The new organization owns no projects initially and appears in the creator's account as an available organization. Includes the 'last administrative-permission holder cannot remove themselves' invariant being enforceable (the constraint is established here even though leave/remove flows live in R-0007).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0004 owns switching between organizations. R-0005 owns sending invitations. R-0007 owns leaving or removing organization members. M-0004 owns roles, granular permissions, organization policy configuration, and organization deletion (B-0067). This node only covers org existence and the creator's bootstrap admin grants.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: creating an org makes the creator a member with admin permissions and the org appears under their account. Positive: creator can invoke the admin-only operations referenced in B-0066 (invite, manage access, configure, set policy). Falsification: creating an org from an unsigned-in session fails. Falsification: a non-creator initially cannot perform admin-only operations on a fresh org.

playbook: From a signed-in account, create a new organization with a chosen name.
Verify the org exists and the creator is a member with the admin permissions B-0066 names.
Verify the org owns no projects.
Sign in as a different account and confirm that account cannot perform admin-only operations on the new org.
Negative walk: attempt to create an org without being signed in; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Organization creation is the precondition for inviting, leaving, deleting, and policy work. Pulling it out before any organization-membership behavior keeps the invitation flow simple to shape.
risks: B-0066 implies but does not specify the org-name uniqueness rule (per-account? globally? namespaced?). Spec shaping must pick one.

nodes.title: Switch the active account
nodes.scope: Multi-account context within one user session. The user can list accounts they are signed into, select any as active, and run multiple windows where each window has its own active account. Switching preserves prior account state so returning resumes without re-authentication. Includes phone parity (the switcher works on phone interfaces).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0001 owns account creation. R-0004 owns organization switching within an account (a different concept). M-0021 owns cross-interface continuity for *which* projects are available; this node only covers the active-account selector itself.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: with two signed-in accounts, switching changes which projects/orgs are available. Positive: two windows can each have a different active account. Positive: returning to a prior account does not require re-authentication. Falsification: switching to an account the user is not signed into fails. Falsification: state from one account leaks into another window's view.

playbook: Sign in as account A; create or select a project. Sign in as account B in a second session; verify A's project is not visible.
Switch the active account in session A from A to B; verify B's projects appear and A's are gone.
Switch back to A; verify A's state resumes without re-authentication.
Open a phone interface; verify the same switch flow is available with no extra steps.
Negative walk: attempt to switch to an account the session has no credentials for; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Account switching is independent of organization concepts — only requires R-0001 — and unblocks any later spec that needs to demonstrate cross-account isolation.

nodes.title: Switch the active organization within an account
nodes.scope: Active-organization selection within one account. When an account belongs to multiple organizations, the user can list them and select any as active. Switching changes which projects are listed and which organization-level policies/configuration apply. Personal accounts (zero orgs) gracefully no-op.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0003 owns account switching. R-0006 owns joining additional orgs (which is a precondition for there being more than one org to switch between, but the switch behavior itself is independent — fixturable). M-0004 owns organization-level policy and configuration; this node only changes which org is active, not the policies themselves.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: with two-org membership, switching changes which projects are listed. Positive: phone interface offers the same switcher. Positive: a personal account (no orgs) sees no organization-scoped actions. Falsification: switching to an org the account doesn't belong to fails. Falsification: switching does not affect projects from a different organization.

playbook: Fixture an account belonging to orgs X and Y. Connect a project to X and a different project to Y.
From the account, switch active org to X; verify only X's project is listed.
Switch to Y; verify only Y's project is listed.
Sign in as a personal account (no org); verify the org-switcher is empty/disabled and no org-scoped actions are exposed.
Negative walk: attempt to switch to an org the account is not a member of; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Org switching is the pair to account switching but at a different scope. Independent enough to ship as its own spec; depends on R-0002 because there must be an org concept to switch into.

nodes.title: Invite a person to an organization
nodes.scope: The invite-creation flow: an admin specifies a recipient identifier and the organization-level permissions they will hold on acceptance, the invitation becomes visible to both sides, and admins can revoke before acceptance. Both new-account acceptance (B-0043's invitation pathway) and existing-account acceptance (R-0006) become possible once invitations exist. Bulk invitations and identity-provider provisioning are out of scope.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0001 owns creating new accounts via accepted invitations. R-0006 owns the existing-account acceptance flow. R-0007 owns leaving/removing members. M-0004 owns granular permission selection (role templates, policy constraints) — this node only stores the chosen permissions on the invitation.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: an admin sends an invitation with chosen permissions; the invitation is visible to admin and recipient. Positive: admin can revoke an unaccepted invitation. Positive: organization-level permissions on the invitation match what the invitee receives on acceptance. Falsification: a non-admin cannot send an invitation. Falsification: a revoked invitation cannot be accepted. Falsification: an invitation in a personal context (non-organizational) cannot be sent.

playbook: As org admin, send an invitation to a fresh identifier with a specific permission set.
Verify the invitation is visible to the admin and revocable.
Have the invitee accept (creating a new account via R-0001's invitation pathway); verify the resulting member holds exactly the granted permissions.
Send a second invitation; revoke it before acceptance; attempt acceptance and verify it fails.
Negative walk: as a non-admin, attempt to send; expect rejection. Attempt to send a personal-context invitation; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Invitation creation is independent of acceptance and is needed before any acceptance flow can be proven end-to-end.
risks: B-0044 leaves invitation transport (email? in-product? both?) out of scope of the behavior. Pick during shaping.

nodes.title: Join an organization with an existing account
nodes.scope: The existing-account acceptance pathway: a user with a Tanren account accepts an invitation, becoming a member of the inviting organization with the invitation's organization-level permissions. The account's other organization memberships are unaffected. After acceptance, the user can switch into the joined org via R-0004 and later leave via R-0007.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0001 owns the new-account-via-invitation pathway. R-0005 owns invitation creation. R-0007 owns leaving. M-0031 (project access) governs project-level access — invitation acceptance does NOT grant project access automatically.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: an existing account accepts a valid invitation and becomes a member with the granted permissions. Positive: other organization memberships are unaffected. Positive: project-level access is not auto-granted. Falsification: accepting an invitation addressed to a different account fails. Falsification: accepting an expired or revoked invitation fails. Falsification: accepting twice produces no duplicate membership.

playbook: Fixture: account A exists; org X has invited account A.
Sign in as A; accept the invitation; verify A is now a member of X with the granted permissions.
Verify A's other org memberships are unchanged.
Verify A has no project access in X (project access requires a separate flow).
Switch active org to X; verify the org is selectable.
Negative walk: attempt to accept an invitation addressed to a different account; expect rejection. Re-accept the same invitation; expect a no-op or already-accepted error, not a duplicate membership.
nodes.rationale: The existing-account pathway needs to be a separate spec from the new-account pathway because the user identity differs (already-known vs new) and the proof structure is different.

nodes.title: Leave or remove an organization member
nodes.scope: Membership departure flows. Voluntary leave (member-initiated) and involuntary removal (admin-initiated) share the same lifecycle event and the 'last administrative-permission holder cannot depart' invariant. In-flight ownership and assignments are surfaced to the actor before the action completes so nothing is silently orphaned. Both flows are attributed and appear in the org's permission change history. Removed/leaving accounts retain their identity and other-org memberships.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0005 owns invitations. R-0006 owns acceptance. M-0004 owns granular admin-permission management and reassignment of in-flight work. M-0042 (change history) is referenced but the change-history view itself is M-0004. This node only emits the events that change history will display.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: a non-last-admin member voluntarily leaves; access ends; account remains. Positive: leaving surfaces in-flight work before completing. Falsification: the last admin cannot leave. Falsification: leaving an org the account is not a member of fails.

Positive: an admin removes a non-last-admin member; access ends; account remains. Positive: removal surfaces in-flight work to the remover. Positive: the removed account is notified. Falsification: a non-admin cannot remove. Falsification: cannot remove the last holder of any administrative permission.

playbook: Fixture: org with admin A and members B, C.
Voluntary: as B, leave the org; verify B's access ends; verify B's account and other org memberships are intact.
Involuntary: as A, remove C; verify C is notified; verify C's access ends.
In-flight visibility: assign B some active work, then attempt to leave; verify the in-flight work is surfaced and the user must acknowledge before leaving.

Negative walks: as A (the only admin), attempt to leave; expect rejection. Attempt to remove A as the last admin; expect rejection. Attempt to remove a member from an org the actor is not in; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Leave (B-0059) and remove (B-0060) share enough structure — same lifecycle event, same in-flight surfacing, same last-admin invariant — that splitting them would force redundant scaffolding. They differ only in actor (self vs admin), which is a single scenario axis in BDD.
risks: What 'in-flight work' means concretely depends on M-0011 (Implementation Loop) and M-0018 (Team Coordination). For this node, surface as a generic list with placeholders; richer surfacing happens when those milestones land.

nodes.title: Manage user-tier configuration and credentials
nodes.scope: Establish the user-tier configuration tier and the user-credentials lifecycle on every declared interface (web, api, mcp, cli, tui). Cover viewing, setting, updating, and removing user-tier configuration values; cover adding and updating user-owned credentials with the security property that stored values are never returned or projected after submission. The two behaviors are bundled because the user-tier configuration management view IS the surface where users add and inspect their credentials — splitting them would force redundant UI/CLI/TUI plumbing.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0009 owns cross-device sync. R-0010 owns notification preferences (which live in user-tier config but are tested as a distinct behavior because they have their own routing and per-event-type structure). R-0011 owns organization-tier configuration and shared secrets. R-0012 owns the credential-usage view (where a credential is used, with no value exposure) — this node covers credential storage and management, not usage tracking. R-0013 owns rotation and revocation. M-0004 owns granular permissions on credential management.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: user views their user-tier values; user updates a preference; user adds a credential and sees its metadata (not its value); user removes a credential. Positive: a teammate signed into the same account-distinct identity does not see the user's user-tier values. Falsification: user attempts to view another account's user-tier values (rejected). Falsification: attempting to set a value with malformed input fails before persistence.

Per declared interface — Positive: after creating a credential, the metadata view shows kind/scope/last-updated but never the value. Positive: API responses, CLI output, MCP tool returns, TUI screens, and web pages all redact the value. Falsification: any attempt to query a credential's stored value through any read path returns redacted/absent — never the raw value. Falsification: logs and audit history contain no raw credential values.

playbook: Web: open user-tier configuration; verify the existing values are listed; update one value; verify the update persists. Add a credential via the UI; verify only metadata is shown afterward; attempt to inspect the value (e.g. via browser network tools); verify the value is not in any response.
API: GET user-tier config; PUT to update a value; GET to verify; POST to add a credential; GET the credential record; verify the response contains metadata only.
CLI: `tanren-cli config user list`, `tanren-cli config user set <key> <value>`, `tanren-cli credential add`, `tanren-cli credential list`. Verify the credential list shows metadata only.
MCP: invoke the user-config and user-credential tools; verify return shapes match the API behavior (metadata only on read).
TUI: open user-tier config screen; navigate to credentials sub-view; add a credential; verify the screen never displays the value after submission.
Cross-interface: add a credential via API; verify it appears in user-tier config on web, cli, tui, mcp — with metadata only on every surface.

Negative walks per interface: attempt to view another user's user-tier values; attempt to read a credential's value via any direct query path; submit invalid credential data. Each must be rejected with a user-readable reason.
Storage hygiene check: dump the postgres event log; grep for the test credential's plaintext value; verify zero matches. Repeat against logs and audit projections.
nodes.rationale: User-tier configuration and the user-credential storage hygiene property are tightly coupled — every credential surface IS a user-tier configuration surface. Splitting them would create two specs that share most of their UI/CLI/TUI plumbing. Bundling lets the BDD scenarios for one behavior naturally exercise the other.
risks: Exact set of user-tier configuration keys is open. The spec's BDD must cover the schema as defined at shaping time; if the schema changes later, scenarios add but don't subtract.

Authentication mechanism (R-0001's lock) interacts with credential storage — if password-based auth is chosen, the password itself is a user credential subject to B-0125's no-exposure rule. Make sure the BDD scenarios exercise this case explicitly.

nodes.title: Configuration follows the account across devices
nodes.scope: Prove that user-tier configuration and credentials persist across devices when the same account signs in from a second device. Includes the property that signing out (or removing the device's session) stops that device from receiving subsequent configuration. Project-tier and organization-tier sync is covered by their respective specs (deferred to wave B for project-tier, R-0011 for org-tier).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0008 owns user-tier configuration and credential lifecycle on a single device. This spec proves that the same lifecycle state is observable from a second device. R-0011 owns organization-tier configuration sync (which is structurally similar but at a different tier). Wave-B project-tier configuration nodes (after M-0003) will prove project-tier sync.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: sign into account A on device 1, set a user-tier preference and add a credential; sign into account A on device 2; verify both the preference and the credential metadata appear without re-entry. Positive: change the preference on device 2; verify the change appears on device 1. Falsification: sign out of device 2; subsequent changes on device 1 do not appear on device 2. Falsification: device 1 cannot read device 2's session-only state.
Cross-device test fixture: spawn two parallel sessions of each interface representing two devices; assert state convergence across them within the freshness target.

playbook: Sign in as account A on a 'device 1' session of each interface. Set user-tier config values; add a credential.
Open a 'device 2' session for each interface; sign in as account A; verify the same values appear without manual re-entry.
Change a value on device 2; observe device 1 receives the change.
Sign out of device 2; change a value on device 1; verify device 2 does NOT receive the change after sign-out.
Negative walk: attempt to read device 1's local session state from device 2's account context; verify isolation.
nodes.rationale: Sync-across-devices is independently testable from the management lifecycle (R-0008) — the proof structure is fundamentally about multi-session convergence, not value editing.
risks: Eventual consistency vs strict consistency for cross-device sync is a real spec-shaping question. The behavior says 'become available' which permits a freshness window; the BDD must pick a freshness target that's testable but not overly strict.
Offline editing and conflict resolution is explicitly out of scope of B-0051; the BDD should not test that case.

nodes.title: Configure notification preferences and routing
nodes.scope: Per-event-type notification preferences (which events I want to be told about), per-channel routing (visual / auditory / push to phone), and per-organization overrides. Proven with at least the organizational-override scope; per-project overrides land in wave B after M-0003 introduces projects. The single-loop muting flow named in B-0004 is supported but completed by M-0021's notifications-owning node.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0008 owns the user-tier configuration framework. This node adds a SPECIFIC settings family (notifications) that has its own per-event/per-channel structure. M-0021 owns the actual notification delivery (B-0004 — a notification reaches the user when a loop completes); this node owns the PREFERENCES that decide whether and how delivery happens.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: user enables/disables a specific event type; user picks channels for an event type; user sets an organization-wide override; user revisits and verifies persistence. Falsification: setting a channel the user's device doesn't support is rejected; setting an org override without the right permission is rejected; settings changes do not retroactively re-deliver notifications already pending.

playbook: Per interface: navigate to notification settings; toggle 'loop completed' on; pick visual + push channels; verify persistence.
Set an organization-scope override that mutes 'walk requested' for one organization; verify the user's other org behavior is unaffected.
Trigger a fixtured event of each kind; verify routing matches the configured channels.
Negative walks: select an unsupported channel; verify rejection. Set an override without permission; verify rejection.
nodes.rationale: Notification preferences are functionally distinct from generic user-tier configuration — they have per-event-type and per-channel structure that doesn't fit the simple key/value model of B-0048. The behavior is its own provable unit.
risks: B-0004's actual delivery is M-0021's responsibility; this node can only fixture events, not exercise the real loop-completion event yet.
Per-project overrides defer to wave B; the BDD should not attempt to prove them without projects existing.

nodes.title: Store organization secrets without exposing values
nodes.scope: Organization-tier secret lifecycle: an admin can add, update, and remove organization-owned secrets; the value is never displayed after submission; access to USE the secret follows organization policy. Proven at organization scope only — project-scope secrets (B-0088) and project-or-org policy enforcement (B-0233) are wave-B / M-0004 work. The 'access follows organization policy' outcome is proven at the level: 'a non-admin org member cannot use a secret reserved for admins'.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0008 owns user-tier credential storage. R-0012 owns the usage-without-revealing-values view. R-0013 owns rotate/revoke. M-0004 owns the granular use-policy (B-0233). Wave-B project-secrets node (after M-0003) extends the storage proof to project tier.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: admin creates an org-tier secret; metadata is visible; value is not displayed after submission. Positive: admin updates the secret; value still not displayed. Positive: admin removes the secret. Positive: a non-admin org member with permitted use can reference the secret in their work but cannot read its value. Falsification: a non-permitted org member cannot use the secret. Falsification: any read path that attempts to return the value is redacted.

playbook: Sign in as org admin (R-0002 admin grant). Per interface, navigate to organization secrets; create a secret named 'test-secret' with a known value.
Verify the secret appears in the org-secret list with metadata only.
Update the secret's value; verify the new value also is not displayed.

Sign in as a non-admin org member (use R-0005 to invite, R-0006 to accept, then revoke admin grant — or use a fixtured non-admin); verify they can reference the secret in their work but cannot read its value.
Storage hygiene: dump postgres event log; grep for the secret value; verify zero matches.
Negative walks per interface: attempt direct value read; attempt secret access from outside the org; attempt creation without admin permission. Each rejected with reason.
nodes.rationale: Organization-tier secret storage is the second-tier counterpart to R-0008's user-tier credential storage. Bundling both in one spec would conflate distinct scope concerns; keeping them split lets each scope's permission and access semantics be proven independently. B-0050 (account/org shared configuration defaults) is `supports_behaviors` because R-0011 partly exercises org-tier configuration plumbing, but B-0050's full proof requires multi-project demonstration which is wave B.
risks: Project-tier secrets (B-0126's project scope) defer to wave B. The BDD must explicitly exercise org-tier scope only and not claim to cover project-scope.
B-0233 (configure credential use policy by scope) is M-0004 work. This node assumes a baseline 'admin can manage, members can use if granted' policy that B-0233 will later let users tune.

nodes.title: See where credentials and secrets are used without revealing values
nodes.scope: Usage-metadata view across both user credentials and organization secrets. The view shows: which work or configuration uses each credential/secret; the freshness of usage data; redaction of any accidentally-included values. Proven on user-tier credentials (R-0008) and organization-tier secrets (R-0011). Project-tier secrets land in wave B; the BDD scenarios should be structured so that project-tier scenarios slot in as additions when M-0003 lands.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0008 / R-0011 own creation. This node owns READING usage metadata. R-0013 owns rotate/revoke (which uses this view to surface 'will this break work?'). R-0014 owns issue detection (which uses this view as input).

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: a user-tier credential is in use by some configuration; the usage view shows which configuration. Positive: an org-tier secret is in use; usage view shows the configuration / scope. Positive: usage records support audit (timestamps, attribution). Falsification: usage view never displays the credential value. Falsification: a user without visibility into the consuming work cannot infer hidden work via usage metadata.

playbook: Per interface: create a user-tier credential (R-0008 fixture); use it in some user-tier configuration. Open the usage view; verify the consuming configuration is listed.
Per interface: create an org-tier secret (R-0011 fixture); use it. Open the usage view; verify the consuming configuration is listed; verify the value is not present.
Audit walk: use the same credential from multiple configurations over time; verify the usage view shows all usages with timestamps and attribution.
Negative walk: as a user with no visibility into a consuming work item, query the usage view; verify the entry is either absent or redacted in a way that doesn't leak the existence of hidden work.
nodes.rationale: Usage-metadata is a distinct READ path from credential creation/deletion. Different proof concerns (audit completeness, redaction correctness) than the storage proof in R-0008/R-0011.
risks: What counts as 'usage' is design-dependent — every read? every reference? A spec-shaping decision must define 'usage' precisely before BDD scenarios can be authored.

nodes.title: Rotate or revoke a credential or secret
nodes.scope: Lifecycle operations: rotate (replace the value while preserving identity) and revoke (mark unusable, work that depended on it surfaces a missing-access error rather than silently using stale access). Proven for both user-tier credentials and organization-tier secrets. Bundled because both operations share the same lifecycle event (a state change on the credential/secret), the same audit attribution, and the same downstream-work surfacing requirement.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0008 / R-0011 own creation. R-0012 owns the read-time usage view. M-0004 owns the granular permission model (who can rotate vs revoke). M-0009 (Provider Integrations) owns the case where revoke must propagate to the external provider — this spec covers Tanren-internal effects only.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: rotate a user credential; the new value replaces the prior; rotation is recorded without exposing either value; users can see rotation occurred. Positive: rotate an org secret; same outcomes. Falsification: rotation by a user without permission is rejected. Falsification: rotation does not return the new or old value through any read path.

Per declared interface — Positive: revoke a user credential; future work using it reports missing access; revocation is attributed and visible in history. Positive: revoke an org secret; same outcomes. Falsification: revocation by an unauthorized user is rejected. Falsification: revoked credentials are not silently usable from any code path.

playbook: Per interface: rotate a user credential; verify the new value replaces the old (test by attempting auth with the old value — should fail); verify rotation appears in audit history.
Per interface: rotate an org secret; verify same.
Per interface: revoke a user credential; trigger work that depended on it; verify a missing-access error is surfaced rather than silent failure.
Per interface: revoke an org secret; trigger work using it; same.
Negative walks: attempt rotation without permission; attempt revocation without permission; attempt to use a revoked credential. All must fail with explicit reasons.
nodes.rationale: Rotate and revoke are two faces of the same credential-lifecycle change — both replace state and require downstream work to surface the change. They share BDD scaffolding but have distinct positive scenarios, so they're bundled rather than split.
risks: Rotation semantics for connection-bound credentials (e.g. database passwords actively in use) is design-dependent. The BDD must pick a model — drain old before rotating, atomic swap, or grace window — and exercise it consistently.
External-provider revoke propagation defers to M-0009. This spec must explicitly exercise only the Tanren-internal effects.

nodes.title: Detect over-scoped or stale credentials
nodes.scope: Two credential-quality detectors that scan available credential / provider metadata and surface issues to authorized users: (1) over-scoped credentials whose granted permissions exceed what their actual usage requires, and (2) expiring, unused, or stale credentials whose freshness has lapsed. Both detectors emit findings the user can review; both are read-only — they do NOT auto-rotate or auto-revoke (those are R-0013's domain).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0012 owns the usage data both detectors consume. R-0013 owns the rotate/revoke remediation actions. M-0004 owns the policy that defines 'over-scoped' and 'stale' thresholds. M-0019 owns the dashboards that aggregate these findings across many credentials.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: a credential with permissions broader than its usage record produces an over-scoped finding. Positive: the finding identifies the gap between granted and used. Falsification: a credential whose granted permissions match usage produces no finding. Falsification: detection respects visibility — an unauthorized user cannot see findings about credentials they can't see.

Per declared interface — Positive: an expired or expiring credential produces a finding. Positive: a credential unused for the configured stale threshold produces a finding. Falsification: a fresh, in-use credential produces no finding. Falsification: detection does not surface secret values in any finding.

playbook: Fixture: create an over-scoped credential (e.g. one with admin grants used only for read-only work). Run the detector. Verify a finding identifies the gap.
Fixture: create a credential with last-used over the stale threshold. Run the detector. Verify a stale finding.
Fixture: create a credential with an expiry date in the near future. Run the detector. Verify an expiring finding.
Per interface, verify the findings are listed with redacted values, attributable, and link to the credentials they concern.
Negative walks: as a user without visibility into the affected credential, query findings; verify isolation. Inspect any finding payload; verify no secret values are present.
nodes.rationale: Over-scoped (B-0231) and stale (B-0232) detectors share a common detection lifecycle (scan metadata → produce findings → surface to user) and the same visibility / redaction concerns. Bundling avoids redundant scanner scaffolding while keeping each detector's positive case distinct.
risks: Threshold for 'stale' (e.g. 90 days unused) is policy. Default ships with this spec; M-0004 lets it be tuned.
Detection is read-only by design. A future spec could add 'auto-remediate when finding accepted', but that's not in B-0231/B-0232's scope.

nodes.title: Choose a deployment posture
nodes.scope: Establish the deployment-posture decision (hosted, self-hosted, local-only) for an account / project / installation. Cover: the user can see the posture before project work is dispatched; Tanren explains which capabilities are available or unavailable for the chosen posture; later runtime and credential choices inherit the selected posture unless the user with permission changes it. Posture is a top-level decision that gates first-run progress to providers (R-0016) and ultimately to ready first project (R-0018).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0016 owns provider selection (which depends on this posture choice). R-0017 owns the umbrella first-run checklist that surfaces this decision among others. M-0010 owns runtime placement that respects this posture. M-0020 owns operational adjustments after a posture is in production.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: user with permission selects a posture; Tanren records and shows the selection; Tanren explains capability availability differences. Positive: changing posture later is recorded with attribution. Falsification: a user without permission cannot change posture. Falsification: setting an unsupported posture value is rejected with a user-readable reason.

playbook: Per interface: as a permitted user, list available postures; verify each shows a capability summary; choose one; verify it is recorded.
Verify subsequent runtime/credential views show the posture's available capabilities and grey out / explain unavailable ones.
Change posture; verify the change is attributed and the new capabilities are reflected.
Negative walks: as an unpermitted user attempt to change posture (rejected); attempt to set an invalid posture value (rejected with explicit reason).
nodes.rationale: Deployment posture is foundational to first-run because it constrains every later runtime and credential decision. Splitting into its own spec lets the BDD test the inheritance/explanation outcomes cleanly without conflating with provider selection.
risks: The exact set of postures (hosted / self-hosted / local-only / others?) is design-dependent. Settle during shaping; the BDD must enumerate the supported set explicitly.

Capability availability per posture is not exhaustively defined here. The behavior says Tanren 'explains' availability — the explanation content depends on which capabilities exist (M-0010 etc). Acceptable starting point: explanation lists the categories of capability and which are unavailable for the chosen posture, without enumerating every M-0010 detail.

nodes.title: Select first-run identity and source-control providers
nodes.scope: First-run provider connection: connect an identity provider (so Tanren can attribute work) and a source-control provider (so Tanren can reach repositories). Cover: connections are visible without exposing secret tokens; Tanren can list which identities and repositories are reachable through the connections; provider-connection failures produce actionable, non-secret error info that doesn't block unrelated setup choices. Bundles identity + SCM provider because both are required for an initial project (B-0139) and they share the OAuth-style flow; full provider-management lifecycle is M-0009 (Provider Integrations).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0015 owns posture (which constrains which providers are valid). R-0017 owns the umbrella first-run checklist.
