R-0019 owns project creation/connection (which uses the SCM provider connected here). M-0009 owns ongoing provider integration management — recovery from auth failures, capability discovery, audit. This node only covers the FIRST-RUN connection flow.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: user connects an identity provider; the connection appears with metadata (no secret values). Positive: user connects an SCM provider; reachable repositories are listed. Positive: provider failure leaves an actionable, non-secret error message. Falsification: secret tokens are not revealed in any read path. Falsification: unrelated setup choices are not blocked when one provider connection fails. Falsification: bypass of provider authorization screens is not possible.

playbook: Per interface: connect a fixtured identity provider via the OAuth-style flow; verify the connection appears with metadata; attempt to read the token; verify redaction.
Per interface: connect a fixtured SCM provider; verify reachable repositories are listed.
Inject a fixtured provider failure; verify an actionable error is surfaced; verify other setup choices remain available.
Cross-interface: connect identity via web; verify the connection appears in cli/tui/mcp/api views with consistent metadata.
Negative walks per interface: attempt to read a token via direct query; attempt to bypass an authorization screen; attempt to connect with malformed input. Each rejected with reason.
nodes.rationale: Identity + SCM provider connection is the gating step before project creation can produce useful work — both providers feed into B-0139 (ready first project). Bundling them keeps the BDD focused on the first-run flow; M-0009 handles the broader provider lifecycle.
risks: Provider OAuth/authorization flow is interactive; the BDD must use fixtured providers that simulate the OAuth callback rather than calling real GitHub/etc.

M-0009 will eventually own provider connection management — at that point this node's scenarios may move to M-0009 or stay here as the first-run-specific subset. For now, this is the only place provider connections exist.

nodes.title: Complete first-run setup
nodes.scope: The first-run umbrella: a checklist that shows which setup decisions remain (posture, providers, project, optionally other readiness items), records completed choices, and routes the user to project creation. Bundles first-run orchestration logic; the individual decisions live in R-0015 / R-0016 / R-0019. The 'reach ready first project' end-state is R-0018 (which depends on this checklist completing).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0015, R-0016, R-0019 each own one decision in the checklist. R-0018 owns the end state where the first project is ready. This node is about the checklist UX — orchestrating which steps remain, persisting completed choices across sessions, and providing a clear handoff to project creation.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: a fresh user sees the checklist with posture/providers/project as pending. Positive: completing a sub-step updates the checklist. Positive: completed choices persist across re-sign-in. Positive: when all required steps complete, the user is routed to project creation/connection. Falsification: completing a step does not silently bypass another required step. Falsification: returning to a completed step preserves the prior choice.

playbook: Per interface: as a fresh user, sign in for the first time; verify the checklist shows posture/providers/project as pending.
Complete posture (R-0015 fixture); verify the checklist updates.
Connect identity + SCM (R-0016 fixture); verify the checklist updates.
Verify the user can proceed to project creation/connection (R-0019).
Sign out and back in; verify the checklist state persists.
Negative walks: attempt to skip required steps via direct API; verify rejection. Re-visit a completed step; verify the prior choice is preserved and editable.
nodes.rationale: B-0136 is genuinely an orchestration / coordinator behavior. Its proof is about the checklist mechanics (what's pending, what's done, what's reachable next) — separable from the individual decisions it tracks.
risks: What constitutes the 'required' set for first-run completion is design-dependent. Posture + providers + first project at minimum; other readiness items (e.g. notification preferences) may be optional. Settle during shaping.

nodes.title: Reach a ready first project
nodes.scope: The end-state of first-run: the first project is selected as active; a per-project readiness view shows which categories of work (product planning, repository analysis, runtime setup, credentials) still need attention; the user can begin discovery/planning/spec creation from the project. The readiness view is forward-pointing — it surfaces what's pending across M-0005, M-0009, M-0010 etc, but does not require those milestones to be implemented (it shows them as 'not yet configured' fixtures).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0017 owns the first-run checklist. R-0019 owns project creation. R-0020 owns project switching. M-0005 (planning), M-0009 (providers), M-0010 (runtime) each populate a readiness category once they land. This node only owns the per-project readiness view that aggregates them — with placeholder content until each milestone fills it in.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: after first-run, the first project is selected as active; the readiness view shows which categories of work need attention; the user can begin discovery / planning / spec creation. Positive: completing a readiness category (fixtured) updates the view. Falsification: implementation work cannot start before required readiness checks pass. Falsification: a non-active project is not shown in the readiness view by default.

playbook: Run through R-0017 to first-run completion, then through R-0019 to create/connect the first project.
Verify the first project is selected as active.
Per interface, view the readiness page; verify it shows planning/repo-analysis/runtime/credentials as pending categories with placeholder explanations.
Fixture-complete one category (e.g. mark planning as done); verify the view updates.
Verify the user can navigate to discovery / planning / spec creation from the project (these may be stubs at this point).
Negative walk: attempt to start an implementation loop before required readiness items pass; verify rejection with a 'readiness incomplete' reason.
nodes.rationale: B-0139 is the user-visible 'first-run is done' moment. Its proof is the readiness view + active-project handoff. Separable from B-0136 (the umbrella checklist) because they show different content at different stages.
risks: The list of readiness categories will grow as later milestones add their own contributions. The BDD scenarios should cover the full category set as of shaping time and accept that scenarios will be added later.
What 'required readiness' means before implementation can start needs settling — initial conservative position: planning + repo analysis + runtime are required; the others are recommended.

nodes.title: Connect an existing repository or create a new project
nodes.scope: Two project-creation pathways that both end with a project registered in the user's account: (1) connecting a repository the user already controls (B-0025), and (2) creating a new repository at the same time as the project registration (B-0026). Cover the rule that one project = one repository (no polyrepo or monorepo-as-one-project setups). Cover that the new/connected project is immediately selectable as active, starts empty (no specs/milestones/initiatives), and that B-0026's repository creation happens at a designated host the user has access to.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0020 owns seeing/switching projects. R-0021 owns disconnect. R-0022 owns joining an existing project that already has context. R-0023 owns the CLI install of Tanren assets into the repo (orthogonal — install is a filesystem op, this is account-level registration). M-0009 owns the SCM provider flow that creates the underlying repo for B-0026; this node uses an SCM provider connection (R-0016) but doesn't manage it.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: connect an existing repo; the project appears in the account; project is selectable as active; the repository is unmodified. Falsification: connecting a repo without access is rejected. Falsification: connecting the same repo twice produces a clear error rather than two project records. Falsification: connection does not retroactively reinterpret prior commits as Tanren activity.

Per declared interface — Positive: create a new project from scratch; a new repository is created at the designated host AND registered as a project in one action; project is selectable as active and starts empty. Falsification: creating a new project at a host the user lacks access to is rejected. Falsification: the new project does not start with seeded specs, milestones, or initiatives.

Per declared interface — Positive: personal-scope project registration succeeds for an authenticated account. Falsification: org-scoped registration is denied when the actor is not a member of the target org.

playbook: Per interface: connect a fixtured existing repo to the active account; verify the project appears, is selectable, and the underlying repository is byte-identical to before.
Per interface: create a new project from scratch with a target host; verify a new repository is created AND a project record exists for it; verify the project is empty.
Verify the one-project-per-repo rule by attempting to connect an already-connected repo; expect a clear error.
Cross-interface: create via API; verify it appears in cli/tui/web/mcp views.

Negative walks per interface: connect without repo access (rejected); create at a host without access (rejected); connect a polyrepo (each repo is its own project — verify by connecting two repos and seeing two projects).
nodes.rationale: B-0025 and B-0026 are the two faces of project creation — the difference is whether the underlying repo already exists. The flows share post-creation state (project record, active selection, empty content) and differ only in their setup step. Bundling lets the BDD test the shared post-state once.
risks: B-0026's 'designated host' implies the user has set up an SCM provider via R-0016. The BDD must fixture provider connections. Real-host integration (e.g. real GitHub) is M-0009 work.
Repo identity (URL? clone-token? some hash?) — what counts as 'the same repo' when checking for duplicate connections — is design-dependent.

nodes.title: See and switch between projects in an account
nodes.scope: Project list view + active-project selector. Cover the list showing every project in the active account with attention indicators (a project needs attention if any spec in it does), aggregation of spec-level attention into project-level indicators, drill-down from project list to a specific spec needing attention, phone-usability of the list, and the active-project switcher. Project switching scopes views (specs, loops, milestones) to the newly selected project and preserves the prior project's state for resume.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0019 owns project creation. R-0021 owns disconnect. M-0007 (Spec Lifecycle) owns the spec-level attention indicators that this view aggregates — at M-0003 time these are fixtured. M-0019 owns richer dashboards / cross-project views; this is the simple per-account project list.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: list shows every project in active account with state summary; attention indicators aggregate spec-level attention; user can drill into a flagged spec. Positive: phone interface remains usable. Falsification: list does not include projects from another account. Falsification: a project with no attention-worthy specs does not show an attention indicator.

Per declared interface — Positive: switching projects updates spec/loop/milestone-scoped views; switching is one-step (no sign-out). Positive: returning to a prior project resumes its prior view state. Falsification: switching to a project the account doesn't have access to is rejected. Falsification: switching does not modify the previously active project's state.

playbook: Fixture: account A has projects P1, P2, P3. Fixture P2 with a spec that needs attention.
Per interface: view the project list for A; verify all three appear; verify P2 shows an attention indicator.
Drill into P2's flagged spec; verify navigation works.
Switch active project from P1 to P3; verify spec/loop views update to P3's content.
Open a phone interface; verify the list and switcher are usable on small screen.
Cross-interface: switch via API; verify other interface views reflect the change.
Negative walks: attempt to switch to a non-account project; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Listing and switching are paired user concerns — the list is where switching is initiated. Bundling reduces UI scaffolding duplication; the BDD covers list-display and switch-effect distinctly within the same spec.
risks: Attention-indicator semantics depend on M-0007's spec lifecycle states. At M-0003 time the indicators are fixtured; the BDD covers the aggregation rule (project flagged iff any spec is) without needing real spec lifecycle logic.

nodes.title: Disconnect a project from Tanren
nodes.scope: Disconnect a project from a Tanren account without modifying or deleting the underlying repository. Cover the precondition that no active implementation loops exist on the project, the outcome that the underlying repo is untouched, the consequence that cross-project dependencies pointing into the disconnected project surface as unresolved (B-0029 is M-0007 territory; here we just emit the unresolved-link signal), and the reconnection-via-B-0025 path that restores access to the prior specs.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0019 owns initial connection. M-0011 (Implementation Loop) owns 'no active loops' enforcement — at M-0003 time we fixture this precondition. M-0007 owns cross-project dependency lookup. M-0020 owns operational/audit aspects of disconnect.

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: disconnect a project with no active loops; project disappears from account views; underlying repo is byte-identical. Positive: cross-project dependencies pointing into the disconnected project surface as unresolved. Positive: reconnect the same repo via R-0019; the prior specs reappear. Falsification: cannot disconnect a project with active loops. Falsification: disconnect does not delete the underlying repo.

playbook: Fixture: project with no active loops; some specs exist; another project has a cross-project dep into this one.
Per interface: disconnect; verify project is gone from views; verify underlying repo on disk/host is unchanged; verify the cross-project dep now shows as unresolved.
Reconnect via R-0019; verify the specs reappear under the same identity.
Negative walks: fixture an active loop; attempt disconnect; expect rejection with a clear reason.
nodes.rationale: Disconnect is a focused single-action behavior with a tight invariant set (repo unchanged, dependencies surfaced, reconnect-able). Worth its own spec because the invariants are non-trivial.
risks: 'No active loops' precondition fixturing is M-0011 work. The BDD here uses a fixture that pretends active loops do/don't exist; once M-0011 lands, real-loop integration scenarios get added.

nodes.title: Join an existing project with current context
nodes.scope: When a team-builder is granted access to a project that already has product/planning/execution/standards context, they can join and see the relevant context immediately without reconstructing it. Cover: showing the project mission, roadmap, active work, relevant standards, and open attention items; distinguishing accepted shared context from drafts/proposals/historical decisions; surfacing missing onboarding context rather than silently assuming it. Bundles the 'access granted' flow assumption — uses R-0006's org-membership flow to deliver project access (project-level access management is M-0004).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0019 owns initial project connection. R-0020 owns project list/switch. M-0004 owns project access grants. M-0005 / M-0007 own the actual planning / spec content this view summarizes. This node owns the JOIN-AND-ONBOARD experience: 'I now have access to this project — what's important right now?'

expected_evidence.description: Per declared interface — Positive: a newly-granted-access user sees mission, roadmap summary, active work, standards, and attention items. Positive: accepted context is visually distinguished from drafts and proposals. Positive: missing context is shown as missing rather than silently elided. Falsification: a user without access cannot view the project context. Falsification: drafts/proposals are not silently shown as accepted.

playbook: Fixture: project with seeded mission, roadmap, active work, standards, attention items, plus some drafts and rejected alternatives.
Grant a fresh team-builder access to the project (via R-0006 fixture).
Per interface: as the new user, view the project; verify mission, roadmap, active work, standards, and attention items are visible.
Verify accepted vs draft/proposal content is visually distinguished.
Fixture a missing-context state (e.g. no mission yet); verify the view shows that explicitly.
Negative walks: attempt to view as a user without access (rejected); attempt to act on a draft as if it were accepted (rejected or warned).
nodes.rationale: Onboarding to an existing project is a distinct user concern from creating one — the proof is about CONTEXT TRANSFER, not project lifecycle. Worth its own spec.
risks: What 'accepted context' looks like depends on M-0005 (planning) and M-0007 (specs). At M-0003 time these are fixtures. Real integration scenarios get added when those milestones land.
Project-level access (vs org-level membership) is M-0004 territory. This spec uses R-0006's org-membership-grants-default-project-access fixture as a stand-in.

nodes.title: Bootstrap Tanren assets into a repository, with selectable agent integrations
nodes.scope: The CLI install flow: from a local repository path and a chosen standards profile, install the methodology assets (commands, agent integrations, standards) into the repo. Two sub-modes bundled because they share most of the install code: (1) install everything for the chosen profile (B-0068), (2) install only selected agent integrations (B-0070). Cover the re-install rules (replaces generated assets, removes stale generated assets, preserves user-edited standards, restores missing standards) and the validation rule (invalid bootstrap input is rejected before any write).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0024 owns drift detection (read-only check on the install output). R-0025 owns the runtime use of installed standards. R-0026 owns upgrade. R-0027 owns uninstall. R-0019 owns the orthogonal account-level project registration (different layer). F-0001 already established the cli binary; this node adds the install subcommand, the profile readers, and the asset writers.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: in a fresh repo, run install with a chosen profile; verify methodology assets, agent-integration assets, and standards files appear. Positive: re-running install replaces generated files, removes stale generated files, preserves user-edited standards, and restores missing standards. Falsification: invalid bootstrap input fails before any file is written. Falsification: re-running install does not modify user-edited standards content.

Positive: install with explicit selection of one or more agent integrations; verify only the selected integrations receive their assets; verify standards required by the profile are still installed. Falsification: an unselected integration's assets are not written. Falsification: invalid integration name is rejected with a clear error.

playbook: Fresh repo: run `tanren-cli install --profile rust-cargo`; verify the expected asset directories appear (commands, .claude/, .codex/, .opencode/, standards).
Edit one of the standards files manually; re-run install; verify the user edit is preserved while generated assets are refreshed.
Delete a generated command file manually; re-run install; verify the file is restored.
Run `install --profile rust-cargo --integrations claude,codex`; verify only those two integration trees are written; verify .opencode/ is absent or unchanged.

Negative walks: pass an invalid profile name; verify failure with no files written. Pass an invalid integration name; verify failure. Edit an asset that's marked 'generated, do not edit'; re-run install; verify it gets overwritten (with a clear changelog if any).
nodes.rationale: Install (B-0068) and selectable-integrations install (B-0070) share the same code path with a parameter difference. Bundling proves both modes via a single spec; splitting would force the same install scaffolding into two specs.
risks: Profile schema (what's in `profiles/rust-cargo` etc) gets locked here. If we want to evolve the profile schema later, expect this spec's BDD to need updating.
Asset 'preservation' rules are subtle (which files are user-editable vs Tanren-generated). The BDD must enumerate the matrix explicitly rather than handwave.

nodes.title: Detect installer drift without mutating files
nodes.scope: A read-only drift check that runs against an installed repository and reports whether generated assets and preserved standards match what a fresh install would produce. Cover: drift in generated assets is reported; missing preserved standards are reported as drift; user-edited preserved standards are accepted as non-drift; the check leaves the repository unchanged.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0023 owns install. R-0026 owns upgrade (which may run drift internally before applying). This node is a read-only sibling — it only reports.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: against a freshly-installed repo, drift is reported as none. Positive: modify a generated asset; drift check reports drift. Positive: delete a preserved standard; drift check reports it as missing. Positive: edit a preserved standard manually; drift check reports it as accepted (not drift). Falsification: the drift check leaves no file modifications behind. Falsification: a missing generated asset is reported as drift even when a preserved standard is unchanged.

playbook: Fresh repo install. Run drift check; verify it reports no drift.
Modify a generated asset (e.g., edit a generated command file). Re-run drift; verify drift is reported with the offending path.
Delete a preserved standard. Re-run drift; verify it's reported as missing.
Edit a preserved standard. Re-run drift; verify it's accepted as non-drift.
After every check, run `git status`; verify the working tree is unchanged by the check itself.
nodes.rationale: Drift is a CLI-only diagnostic. Distinct from install (mutating) and upgrade (mutating with preview). Worth its own spec because the read-only invariant is a security/safety property worth proving on its own.
risks: The drift check's notion of 'matching' is design-dependent (byte-identical? canonical-form-identical?). Settle during shaping.

nodes.title: Use the repository's installed standards
nodes.scope: After Tanren assets are installed in a repo, Tanren commands that need standards must read them from the repo's configured standards location, succeed when present, fail explicitly when missing, and never fall back to unrelated content. Covers the integration between the install output (R-0023) and the runtime use of standards by Tanren commands. The actual Tanren commands that consume standards mostly come later (M-0005 planning, M-0013 quality controls etc); this spec proves the loading and failure-mode contract using a representative consumer.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0023 owns writing standards. R-0024 owns drift detection. M-0005 / M-0013 own commands that ultimately consume standards. This node is the integration bridge: it proves a Tanren command can load and use the installed standards.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: with installed standards present, a representative Tanren command that depends on standards succeeds and reflects them in its output. Falsification: with installed standards missing, the same command fails explicitly with a 'standards missing' message. Falsification: with installed standards present but malformed, the command fails with a parse error rather than substituting unrelated content.

playbook: Install a profile in a fresh repo (R-0023). Run a representative Tanren command that depends on standards (e.g. a lint or format check that reads the standards). Verify it succeeds.
Delete the standards directory. Re-run the command. Verify it fails with an explicit 'standards not found at <path>' message.
Restore standards but corrupt one file. Re-run. Verify a parse error referencing the bad file (not a silent fallback to defaults).
Move standards to a non-default location and update the configured path. Re-run. Verify the command finds them at the new path.
nodes.rationale: B-0071 is the integration test for install — it proves the install output is actually loadable and used. Distinct from R-0023 (which proves the install writes correctly).
risks: Choosing the 'representative consumer' command is design-dependent. If no real consumer command exists at this point in the roadmap, fixture a stub command in the cli that loads standards and reports them — its failure modes are what's actually tested.

nodes.title: Upgrade installed Tanren assets
nodes.scope: The CLI upgrade flow: replace installed Tanren-generated assets with newer versions while preserving user-owned files. Cover the preview-before-apply step (user can see what will change), the protection of user-owned files (they remain untouched), and the reporting of compatibility/migration concerns before destructive changes.
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0023 owns initial install. R-0024 owns drift (which upgrade may use internally). R-0027 owns uninstall. M-0009 owns external agent tool upgrades — this spec is Tanren assets only.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: in a repo installed with version N, run upgrade to version N+1; preview shows the diff; user confirms; generated assets are updated; user-owned files are unchanged. Positive: compatibility/migration concerns are reported before any destructive change. Falsification: upgrade does not modify user-owned files. Falsification: upgrade does not proceed past the preview without explicit confirmation.

playbook: Install version N (fixture or actual previous version). Edit a user-owned standard.
Run `tanren-cli upgrade`. Verify a preview shows what will change. Verify the preview leaves the repo untouched.
Confirm the upgrade. Verify generated assets reflect version N+1; verify the edited standard is preserved.
Inject a fixtured 'migration concern' (e.g. a renamed standards key). Verify it's reported in the preview.
Negative walks: cancel at preview; verify the repo is unchanged. Run upgrade in a repo with no install; expect a clear 'nothing to upgrade' message.
nodes.rationale: Upgrade is structurally similar to install but with a pre-existing state to migrate from and a preview-confirm gate. The proof concerns (preserving user work, reporting migration impact) are distinct enough to warrant a separate spec.
risks: Versioning the asset bundles is design-dependent. The BDD needs at least two version snapshots to fixture; settle the versioning scheme during shaping.
Migration concerns can range from 'renamed file' to 'incompatible profile schema'. The BDD must enumerate the categories of concern.

nodes.title: Uninstall Tanren assets without deleting user work
nodes.scope: The CLI uninstall flow: remove Tanren-generated assets from a repository while preserving user-owned files (specs, source signals, edited standards). Cover the preview-before-apply step that makes destructive effects visible before confirmation. Out of scope: deleting hosted account/project history (that's R-0021's territory) and removing external tracker issues / PRs (M-0015 / M-0009 territory).
nodes.boundary_with_neighbors: R-0021 owns disconnecting a project from the Tanren account (orthogonal — uninstall is filesystem, disconnect is account-level). R-0023 owns install. R-0026 owns upgrade.

expected_evidence.description: Positive: in an installed repo with user-owned files, run uninstall; preview lists what will be removed; user confirms; generated assets are removed; user-owned files (including edited standards) remain. Falsification: uninstall does not delete user-owned files. Falsification: uninstall does not proceed past the preview without explicit confirmation. Falsification: uninstall does not delete hosted account or project history (that path is not exercised here).

playbook: Install in a fresh repo. Edit a user-owned standard. Add a fixtured user file (e.g. a spec) outside Tanren-managed paths.
Run `tanren-cli uninstall`. Verify a preview lists generated assets to remove and confirms user files are preserved.
Confirm. Verify generated assets are removed; verify edited standards remain; verify the user file is untouched.
Negative walks: cancel at preview; verify nothing is removed. Run uninstall in a repo with no install; verify a clear 'nothing to uninstall' message.
nodes.rationale: Uninstall is a focused destructive op with a clear invariant (user work preserved). Worth its own spec.
risks: Determining which files are 'user-owned' vs 'Tanren-generated' depends on the install's record-keeping. The install (R-0023) must produce a manifest that uninstall consumes; settle the manifest format during shaping.

nodes.title: Manage project methodology settings
nodes.scope: Project-tier methodology configuration (active profile, available commands, standards location, local working rules) on every declared interface. The settings are scoped to one project and visible to project members; edits are policy-gated and recorded in the project's change history.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: view methodology settings, change a setting (with permission), see change in subsequent work. Falsification: change without permission rejected; change persists across sessions.

playbook: Per interface, view active methodology profile + standards location for a fixtured project; verify content.
With permission, update a setting; verify it applies to subsequent project commands.
Without permission, attempt the same; verify rejection.
Verify the change appears in the project's change history (R-0032).
nodes.rationale: Project-tier methodology configuration is its own settings family — distinct from runtime defaults (R-0029) and verification gates (R-0030).

nodes.title: Manage project runtime defaults
nodes.scope: Project-tier runtime defaults (default execution target, harness preferences, runtime placement preferences) that apply to project work unless explicitly overridden. Settings are policy-gated and recorded in the project's change history.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: view + edit runtime defaults; new project work uses the new defaults. Falsification: edit without permission rejected; runtime placement that conflicts with org-tier policy is rejected with a clear reason.

playbook: View runtime defaults; verify the displayed values.
Update the default execution target; verify subsequent project work uses it.
Set a runtime preference that violates org policy (R-0043 fixture); verify rejection.
Verify changes appear in change history.
nodes.rationale: Runtime defaults are a distinct settings family from methodology — different proof concerns (placement / target choice vs methodology profile).

nodes.title: Manage project verification gates
nodes.scope: Project-tier verification gate configuration (which gates run, command lines, expected exit semantics, gate ordering). Per the methodology, gates are baseline and only tunable within policy. Edits are policy-gated and recorded.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: view configured gates; tune a gate within allowed bounds; gate runs with new config in subsequent work. Falsification: removing a baseline gate is rejected unless governance exception is in place; invalid gate command rejected before save.

playbook: View configured gates; verify defaults match the methodology profile.
Tune an allowed parameter; verify subsequent task / candidate validation uses the tuned config.
Attempt to remove a baseline gate; verify rejection.
Negative walks: provide an invalid gate command; verify validation fails before save.
nodes.rationale: Verification gates are policy-bounded and have their own validation (gate-command syntax, baseline rules) distinct from generic settings.

nodes.title: Manage project-scoped secrets
nodes.scope: Project-tier secret lifecycle (add / update / remove) using the same no-value-exposure invariant established for organization secrets in R-0011. Project secrets are accessible to project members per project policy and isolated from non-members.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: project member with permission adds / updates / removes a project secret; value never displayed after submission; project members can use it but non-members cannot. Falsification: non-member attempts access (rejected); value never returned in any read path.

playbook: Per interface, as project member with permission, create a project secret; verify metadata-only display.
As another project member, use the secret; verify access works without value exposure.
As a non-member of the project, attempt access; verify rejection.
Storage hygiene: dump event log; grep secret value; verify zero matches.
nodes.rationale: Project-scoped secrets extend R-0011's organization-tier proof to the project tier. The storage and no-exposure code path is shared; the access boundary is project-membership instead of org-membership.

nodes.title: See project configuration change history
nodes.scope: An audit-history view scoped to one project's configuration changes (methodology, runtime defaults, verification gates, secrets metadata). Each entry shows what changed, who made the change, and when. Secret values never appear; only metadata changes (rotated, revoked, scope changed, etc).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: changes from R-0028..R-0031 appear in the history with attribution and timestamps. Positive: history scrolls / paginates over time. Falsification: secret values never appear in history entries; users without project visibility cannot view the history.

playbook: Run a sequence of fixtured changes (methodology setting, runtime default, gate config, secret rotation) on a project.
Per interface, open the project's config change history; verify all changes appear with attribution and order.
Inspect any secret-related entry; verify only metadata is shown.
As a non-member, attempt to view history; verify rejection.
nodes.rationale: The history view consumes events emitted by R-0028..R-0031; depending on those nodes ensures the proof can demonstrate end-to-end audit.

nodes.title: Manage shared configuration defaults across projects
nodes.scope: Account-or-organization-scope defaults that apply across multiple projects (deployment posture, runtime preferences, provider defaults, project-creation defaults). Defaults are visible to scope members and apply to subsequent work on every project in scope; in-flight loops continue under their starting settings. Multi-project demonstration requires at least two projects.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: scope-permitted user views and edits shared defaults; the change applies to two or more projects in the scope. Positive: in-flight loop continues under prior settings. Falsification: edit without permission rejected; per-project override forbidden by policy is rejected.

playbook: Fixture an account or org with two projects.
Per interface, set a shared default (e.g. default execution target) at the scope; verify both projects inherit it.
Start a fixtured loop; change the default; verify the in-flight loop is unaffected.
Override the default at one project's tier (R-0029); verify both states coexist.
Negative walks: edit without permission, attempt forbidden override, attempt cross-organization defaults.
nodes.rationale: Multi-project defaults are a fundamentally different proof from per-project settings — the inheritance and 'apply to subsequent work' rules are unique.

nodes.title: Configure shared project defaults for an account
nodes.scope: Account-tier defaults that newly-created or newly-connected projects inherit (project setup template, runtime preferences, provider mappings, optional integrations). Each project displays which defaults it inherited and which are project-specific. Changes apply only to subsequent setup unless deliberately reapplied.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: account holder defines defaults; new project (R-0019) inherits them; project shows which are inherited vs specific. Positive: change defaults; existing projects unaffected unless reapplied. Falsification: secret values cannot be set in defaults; cross-account defaults are not allowed.

playbook: Set account-tier defaults; create a new project; verify inheritance and the inherited-vs-specific marker.
Change a default; verify existing projects are unaffected.
Apply the change to an existing project deliberately; verify the project picks up the new default.
Negative walk: attempt to put secret values in defaults; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Account-shared project defaults vs organization-shared (R-0033) differ in scope and policy. Account-tier covers solo + multi-project personal use; org-tier covers shared team setup. Both proven.

nodes.title: Distinguish personal credentials from shared secrets
nodes.scope: Cross-tier visual / typed distinction between user-tier credentials, project-tier secrets, and organization-tier secrets. Tanren identifies the tier of every visible access material; secret values stay hidden while scope, owner, usage, and last-updated metadata remain visible. Work that needs unavailable access explains which scope of credential or secret is missing.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: a list of access material shows user-tier, project-tier, and organization-tier entries with clear typed/visual distinction. Positive: when work fails for missing access, the failure names the missing scope. Falsification: shared secret cannot be presented as personal credential or vice versa.

playbook: Fixture user-tier creds (R-0008), org-tier secrets (R-0011), and project-tier secrets (R-0031).
Per interface, view the combined list; verify each entry's tier is displayed clearly.
Trigger work that requires access at each tier; verify the work succeeds when present and fails with tier-specific reason when absent.
Negative walks: attempt to mark a personal credential as shared via direct API; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: B-0199's outcomes explicitly span user-tier + project-tier + org-tier. This node depends on all three credential storage nodes (R-0008, R-0011, R-0031) because the proof must demonstrate the distinction across all three.

nodes.title: See who has access to a project
nodes.scope: Read-only visibility of every person with access to a project, with each person's visibility scope and specific permissions. Access is visible to every project member (no hiding). Direct grants and role-template grants are distinguished. Change history is reachable from this view (links to R-0042).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: project member views the access list; sees every other member with permission detail and grant source (direct vs role). Falsification: a non-member of the project cannot view the access list. Falsification: nobody's access is hidden from project members.

playbook: Fixture a project with several members holding different permission combinations (some via direct grant, some via role template).
Per interface, as a member, open the access list; verify all members are visible with permissions and grant source.
Click into change history; verify R-0042 link works.
Negative walk: as a non-member, attempt to view; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Read access (B-0031) and write access (B-0202) are split because the proof concerns differ — transparency / completeness for read, governance / mutation for write.

nodes.title: Manage project access
nodes.scope: Add a person with a chosen visibility and permission set, modify or revoke an existing person's access, grant individual permissions or apply a role template. Every change is attributed and visible in the project's change history. Treats roles as grant-time templates, not authorization identities.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: permitted user adds / modifies / revokes member access; changes appear in the access list and history. Positive: role template grants its bundled permissions at apply time. Falsification: unpermitted user cannot manage access; org policy that constrains project access cannot be bypassed.

playbook: As a project admin (with manage-access permission), add a new member with a chosen permission set.
Apply a role template to a member; verify they receive the bundled permissions as direct grants.
Modify a member's permission; verify it changes.
Revoke access; verify the member loses visibility.
Negative walk: attempt manage-access without permission; attempt to grant a permission constrained by org policy.
nodes.rationale: Manage (B-0202) is the mutation companion to See (B-0036) — paired but distinct operations.

nodes.title: See existing members' access to an organization
nodes.scope: Read-only visibility of every member of an organization with their organization-level permissions. Every member can see the full member list (no hiding). Direct grants vs role-template grants are distinguished. Project access within the org is managed separately per project (R-0036/R-0037); org-level membership does not itself grant project access.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: org member views the org member list; sees each member with org-level permissions and grant source. Falsification: a non-member of the org cannot view the list. Falsification: org membership alone does not appear as 'project access'.

playbook: Fixture org with several members holding different org permission sets.
Per interface, as a member, view the list; verify members + permissions + grant source visible.
Verify org membership does NOT grant access to any of the org's projects (cross-check with R-0036).
Negative walk: as a non-member, attempt view; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Org access visibility is structurally similar to project access (R-0036) but at a different scope with different invariants (project access is independent).

nodes.title: Manage organization member access
nodes.scope: Modify an existing organization member's organization-level permissions, grant individual permissions or apply role templates. Excludes inviting new members (R-0005), removing members (R-0007), and project-level access (R-0037). Changes are attributed and appear in org change history.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: permitted user modifies an existing member's org permissions; org change history records the change. Falsification: unpermitted user cannot modify; cannot grant project access via this view; cannot invite new members or remove existing members from this view.

playbook: As org admin, modify a member's org permission set; verify the change applies and appears in org change history.
Apply a role template to a member; verify the bundled permissions become direct grants at that moment.
Negative walks: attempt without permission; attempt to grant project access via this view (rejected with explicit reason); attempt to remove member via this view (rejected, redirected to R-0007).
nodes.rationale: Manage (B-0203) and See (B-0038) split for the same reason as project-tier — different proof concerns.

nodes.title: See my own permissions
nodes.scope: Self-introspection: per project and per organization the user has access to, list of permissions currently held with the source of each permission (direct grant vs role template) and the source of any policy constraint (e.g. org policy adjusting effective permissions). Available on every supported interface, including phone.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: user sees per-project / per-org effective permissions with source of each. Positive: when an org policy constrains a permission, the constraint is shown as a reason. Positive: phone access works. Falsification: user cannot see another user's permissions through this view; this view is informational only and does not grant requests.

playbook: Fixture a user with mixed permission sources (direct + role + policy-constrained).
Per interface, view 'my permissions'; verify each permission shows its source.
On phone interface, verify the view is usable.
Negative walk: attempt to query another user's permissions through this endpoint; expect rejection or explicit redirect to R-0036/R-0038.
nodes.rationale: Self-introspection is a fundamentally different concern from 'see who else has access' — this is 'what can I do?', the others are 'who else can do what?'

nodes.title: Manage roles as permission templates
nodes.scope: Roles are grant-time convenience templates that bundle permissions for granting in one action. Applying a role grants its bundled permissions as individual grants AT THAT MOMENT — subsequent role edits do not retroactively change those grants. Authorization checks resolve on permissions only, never on role identity.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: create / edit / delete a role with a permission set. Positive: applying a role grants individual permissions; later editing the role does not change prior grants. Falsification: authorization check refers to a permission, never to a role; role deletion does not orphan permissions already granted via that role.

playbook: Create a role 'Reviewer' with a permission set; apply it to a user.
Verify the user has the individual permissions.
Edit the role to add a permission; verify the existing grant is unchanged.
Apply the edited role to a different user; verify the new user has the additional permission.
Delete the role; verify both users still have their previously-granted permissions.
Negative walk: attempt to use the role as an authorization principal; verify all access checks resolve on permissions only.
nodes.rationale: Roles-as-templates is a methodologically important design choice. The proof must specifically demonstrate non-retroactivity and permission-not-role authorization.

nodes.title: See permission and configuration change history for a project or organization
nodes.scope: Audit history of permission and configuration changes scoped to a project or organization. Each entry shows what changed, by whom, when. History is visible to scope members per project / org policy. Excludes per-user-tier history (which is implicit in R-0008 events).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: scope member views the change history; entries from R-0037 / R-0039 / R-0028..R-0031 appear with attribution. Falsification: non-member cannot view; secret values never appear in history.

playbook: Run a fixtured sequence of access + config changes on a project and on an organization.
Per interface, view the project's history; verify all changes appear in time order.
View the org's history; verify org-level changes appear.
Negative walk: as a non-member, attempt view; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: The history view is the audit surface that other governance behaviors emit events into. Worth its own spec because the read pattern (paginated chronological with attribution) is distinct from the mutation flows.

nodes.title: Configure organization approval policy
nodes.scope: Org-level policy that requires approvals for sensitive actions (e.g. revoking a credential, deleting a project, changing org-tier policy). Policy authoring with permitted approvers, scope of action, satisfaction criteria. Edits to policy are themselves policy-gated and recorded.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: org admin defines a policy that requires N approvals for action X; subsequent attempts of X surface the approval requirement. Falsification: edit without permission rejected; approvers outside permitted set cannot satisfy the policy.

playbook: As org admin, define an approval policy for a sensitive action.
Trigger the action; verify the approval requirement is surfaced (R-0044 / M-0024 will exercise the satisfaction flow more deeply).
Edit the policy; verify changes are recorded.
Negative walks: edit without permission; trigger action with approver outside the permitted set.
nodes.rationale: Approval policy is the configuration surface that M-0024 (autonomy controls) and M-0014 (walk-and-merge) consume. This node owns just the policy authoring; downstream nodes own enforcement.

nodes.title: Test policy changes before applying them
nodes.scope: Dry-run mode for policy edits: simulate the proposed policy against a recent window of fixtured or real actions and report what would have been allowed / denied / required-approval. Helps prevent accidentally locking out work or leaving sensitive paths unguarded.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: org admin proposes a policy edit; runs the dry-run; sees a report of which past actions would have been allowed / denied / approval-gated. Falsification: dry-run does not actually mutate live policy; non-admin cannot run dry-run on policies they cannot edit.

playbook: Define an existing policy. Propose an edit (e.g. tighter approval requirements).
Run the dry-run against a fixtured window of actions; verify the report distinguishes allowed / denied / approval-required.
Inspect live policy; verify it is unchanged.
Apply the edit; verify subsequent actions match the dry-run prediction.
Negative walk: non-admin attempts dry-run on a non-editable policy; verify rejection.
nodes.rationale: Test-before-apply prevents lockouts. The proof is specifically about the 'preview that doesn't mutate' invariant.

nodes.title: Explain why policy denied an operation
nodes.scope: When policy denies an action, surface a user-readable explanation: which policy applied, which permission was required, what the user has, why the gap. Excludes leaking secrets or hidden information through denial details.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: a policy-denied action returns a structured denial reason citing the specific policy and the missing requirement. Falsification: denial does not reveal hidden resources; denial does not enumerate every permission in the universe.

playbook: Fixture a policy-denied action; per interface, attempt the action; verify a user-readable explanation is returned.
Verify the explanation cites which policy applied and what was required.
Inspect denial details; verify they do not leak hidden resource names.
Negative walks: confirm denial does not enumerate every possible permission.
nodes.rationale: Denial explanation is the user-facing audit surface for policy. Worth its own spec because the leak-prevention invariant is non-trivial.

nodes.title: Configure credential use policy by scope
nodes.scope: Policy that constrains how credentials can be used (intended-use binding: e.g. a credential is only usable by certain harnesses, certain phases, or certain subjects). Separate from credential management. Policy is scoped (user / project / org) and policy-gated.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: admin configures use-policy for a credential (e.g. only usable by harness X for phase Y); use outside policy is denied with a clear reason. Falsification: edit without permission rejected; use within policy is allowed.

playbook: Configure a use-policy for a credential at user / project / org tier.
Trigger work that uses the credential within the policy; verify success.
Trigger work that uses it outside the policy; verify denial with explanation.
Negative walk: edit policy without permission; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Use-policy is distinct from credential storage (M-0002) and from approval policy (R-0043). It controls the runtime use boundary of a credential.

nodes.title: Create and manage service accounts
nodes.scope: Non-human actors with their own permissions, separate from the user who creates them. Lifecycle (create / scope / disable / delete) plus visibility of service-account permissions. Service accounts cannot create other service accounts unless granted that permission explicitly.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: org admin creates a service account with a permission set; service account can act under those permissions. Falsification: service account cannot escalate beyond its grants; deleting a service account revokes its access.

playbook: As org admin, create a service account with specific permissions.
Authenticate as the service account; perform actions within its grants; verify success.
Attempt actions outside its grants; verify denial.
Disable the service account; verify subsequent actions fail.
Negative walks: service account attempts to create another service account without permission (rejected); service account attempts to escalate.
nodes.rationale: Service accounts are first-class actors in the policy model. Their lifecycle has distinct invariants from human accounts (no email, no human identity, scoped lifetime).

nodes.title: Create, scope, rotate, and revoke API keys
nodes.scope: API keys associated with a user or service account, scoped (project / organization / capability). Creation issues the secret value at creation time only. Rotation issues a replacement secret; old one becomes invalid. Revocation makes the key immediately unusable. Bundles create + rotate + revoke because they share the same lifecycle and audit pattern.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: create an API key with chosen scope; secret value returned ONCE at creation; usage works within scope. Falsification: secret value not retrievable after creation; usage outside scope denied.

Per interface — Positive: rotate an API key; new secret returned once; old secret rejected. Positive: revoke; subsequent uses rejected. Falsification: rotate / revoke without permission rejected; revoked key cannot be silently re-enabled.

playbook: Create an API key with project-scope; verify the secret is returned once.
Use the key; verify success.
Rotate; receive new secret; verify old secret is rejected and new one accepted.
Revoke; verify subsequent uses are rejected.
Negative walks: attempt to retrieve secret after creation; attempt rotate / revoke without permission.
nodes.rationale: API key lifecycle is shared between create (B-0221) and rotate-revoke (B-0274). Bundling avoids redundant lifecycle scaffolding.

nodes.title: See API key and service account usage
nodes.scope: Usage records for service accounts and API keys: when used, by what action, against what resource. Supports audit and troubleshooting. Excludes the secret value itself in any audit entry.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: usage view shows recent uses of a service account / API key with timestamps and action attribution. Positive: usage records survive rotation (continuity across rotations). Falsification: secret value never appears in any usage entry.

playbook: Use a service account and an API key against several actions.
Per interface, view their usage; verify entries with timestamps.
Rotate the API key; verify usage records persist across the rotation boundary.
Negative walk: attempt to view another tenant's usage; expect rejection.
nodes.rationale: Usage tracking for machine identities is a distinct audit concern from credential usage (R-0012). The continuity-across-rotation property is specific to long-lived machine identities.

nodes.title: Delete an organization with project disposition
nodes.scope: Organization deletion with required project disposition (transfer, archive, or delete-with-explicit-confirmation per project owned by the org). Members are notified; pending invitations are cancelled; org ceases to exist; member accounts persist. Bundles B-0067 (delete) + B-0272 (disposition decision) because they're inseparable.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: with permitted user and resolved project disposition, deletion proceeds; org is gone; members retain accounts. Falsification: deletion without permission rejected; deletion without resolving every project's disposition rejected.
Per interface — Positive: each project owned by the org gets a disposition (transfer / archive / delete) before deletion proceeds. Falsification: missing disposition for any project blocks deletion.

playbook: Fixture an org with several projects + members + pending invitations.
As authorized user, attempt deletion; verify the disposition step appears.
Resolve each project's disposition (transfer some, archive some, delete some).
Confirm deletion; verify members are notified, invitations cancelled, org gone, accounts intact, projects follow chosen dispositions.
Negative walks: attempt without permission; attempt without resolving every project's disposition.
nodes.rationale: B-0067 and B-0272 are inseparable per B-0067's behavior text — disposition is required before deletion. Bundling avoids two specs that must be co-developed anyway.

nodes.title: Turn a vague idea into a project brief
nodes.scope: Discovery entry point: take an unstructured idea (a paragraph of text, voice transcription, or imported context) and produce a structured project brief artifact stored under docs/product/. Brief is editable iteratively; supports the rest of the discovery cluster (R-0052..R-0055).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: paste or pipe in a vague idea; receive a structured brief; iterate by editing. Falsification: empty input rejected; brief artifact is project-scoped (not visible to other projects).

playbook: Per interface, submit a vague idea text against a fresh project; verify a brief is produced with structured sections (problem, vision, scope sketch).
Iterate by editing; verify changes persist.
Negative walks: empty input; cross-project visibility check.
nodes.rationale: Discovery entry point — every later discovery behavior refines the brief produced here.

nodes.title: Define target users and problems
nodes.scope: Add target user descriptions and the problems they face to the project brief. Each user gets a description; each problem links to one or more users. Stored under docs/product/personas.md (project-scoped) or in a brief structure.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: add target users with descriptions; add problems linked to users; brief is updated. Falsification: invalid persona slug rejected; problem without a target user is flagged.

playbook: Add target users to a brief; verify they appear.
Add problems linked to users; verify the linkage.
Negative walks: invalid slug, dangling problem.
nodes.rationale: Target users + problems is its own discovery step — distinct artifact from constraints / non-goals / success signals.

nodes.title: Define product non-goals and constraints
nodes.scope: Add explicit non-goals (what the product is NOT) and constraints (commitments / boundaries) to the project brief. Both are first-class to prevent scope creep.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: add a non-goal and a constraint; both appear in the brief. Falsification: removing or editing without permission rejected; non-goals and constraints are visually distinguished from goals.

playbook: Add non-goals; verify they're labeled as non-goals.
Add constraints; verify they're labeled as constraints.
Edit / remove with permission; without permission verify rejection.
nodes.rationale: Non-goals + constraints share authoring patterns and discovery context — distinct from target users (R-0052) and success signals (R-0054).

nodes.title: Define product success signals
nodes.scope: Capture how the project knows it's succeeding: qualitative signals (user delight, fewer support cases) and quantitative metrics (DAU, completion rate). Signals are linked to specific problems / target users when applicable.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: define success signals (qualitative + quantitative) tied to problems / users from R-0052. Falsification: dangling signals (no linkage and no rationale) are flagged.

playbook: Define a quantitative metric; verify it appears with a target value if specified.
Define a qualitative signal; verify it can be linked to a problem.
Add a dangling signal; verify it's flagged for review.
nodes.rationale: Success signals close the discovery loop — every later observation milestone (M-0019) eventually reports against these.

nodes.title: Import existing planning context
nodes.scope: Import an existing planning artifact (a Notion doc, a Google Doc, a markdown file, or an outline from a prior tool) and convert into a starting brief structure. Imported text is clearly marked as 'imported, needs review' until accepted.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: import a fixtured planning doc; structured brief sections are populated as imported drafts. Falsification: imported text is not silently accepted as canon; cross-project import is sandboxed.

playbook: Import a fixtured doc; verify sections populate as drafts.
Accept some sections; verify they become accepted brief content.
Negative walks: import without permission; cross-project visibility.
nodes.rationale: Import is a separate flow from authoring — different proof concerns (no-silent-acceptance, sandboxing of imported text).

nodes.title: Bootstrap a product mission for a project
nodes.scope: Synthesize a one-paragraph product mission from the brief (target users, problems, success signals, constraints). Mission is the durable north-star statement that B-0098 ('keep work aligned with mission') checks against. Editable iteratively.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: synthesize a mission from a brief; mission is stored under docs/product/. Falsification: mission cannot be set without a brief; cross-project missions are isolated.

playbook: With a populated brief, synthesize a mission; verify content.
Edit; verify persistence.
Negative walks: no brief; cross-project access.
nodes.rationale: Mission is the durable summary the work-alignment guard (R-0058) checks against. Its own spec because the synthesis-from-brief step is distinct.

nodes.title: Maintain a product roadmap as planning context
nodes.scope: User-facing roadmap CONTEXT (high-level themes, target dates, sequencing) authored by the product team. Distinct from the executable roadmap DAG (which is craft-roadmap territory). This is the human narrative that feeds craft-roadmap; that DAG synthesis lives elsewhere in M-0005 conceptually but is the core deliverable of this milestone via the dag.json itself.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: author + edit + view roadmap context themes; changes are recorded. Falsification: edit without permission rejected; cross-project isolation.

playbook: Author a few roadmap themes for a fixtured project.
Reorder; edit dates; verify persistence.
Negative walks: permission, cross-project.
nodes.rationale: Human-readable roadmap is distinct from the machine-readable DAG — different artifact, different proof.

nodes.title: Keep work aligned with product mission
nodes.scope: An alignment guard: when shaping or accepting work, surface whether the work aligns with the project's product mission and flag drift. Doesn't block work — the user decides — but makes alignment visible.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: shape a spec aligned with mission; alignment is shown as positive. Positive: shape a spec that drifts; drift is flagged. Falsification: the guard does not silently block work; user can override with rationale.

playbook: With mission set, shape a spec that aligns; verify positive alignment indicator.
Shape a spec that drifts; verify the drift flag.
Override the flag with rationale; verify the rationale is recorded.
Negative walk: confirm the guard never silently blocks.
nodes.rationale: Alignment guard is its own user-visible behavior — distinct from mission authoring (R-0056).

nodes.title: Maintain the accepted behavior catalog
nodes.scope: Behavior catalog CRUD: add a behavior in draft, accept it, deprecate, supersede. Catalog is project-scoped under docs/behaviors/. Each behavior carries the schema we already established (frontmatter + intent + preconditions + outcomes + scope + related). The identify-behaviors flow lives here.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: draft / accept / deprecate / supersede a behavior; catalog updates. Positive: behaviors enforce the schema (interfaces in {web, api, mcp, cli, tui}; personas valid; etc.). Falsification: invalid schema rejected before save; deprecating an accepted behavior preserves the tombstone.

playbook: Draft a behavior; accept it; verify it appears in the catalog.
Deprecate via supersede chain; verify the successor's supersedes field references the deprecated ID.
Negative walks: invalid schema, missing required field, illegal interface ID.
nodes.rationale: Behavior catalog maintenance is the spine of identify-behaviors. Worth its own spec because the schema-validation rules are non-trivial.

nodes.title: See behavior coverage and verification status
nodes.scope: A view of every accepted behavior with its current verification status (unimplemented / implemented / asserted / retired) and which spec node completes it (when present). Helps planners see gaps and prioritize.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: view shows every accepted behavior with status + completing spec node. Positive: filter by status / area / personas. Falsification: a behavior whose status changed without proof is flagged for review (asserted requires active BDD).

playbook: View the coverage; verify status icons / columns are present.
Filter by status (unimplemented); verify the list narrows.
Mark a behavior asserted without proof (fixture); verify the flag fires.
nodes.rationale: Coverage view is the user-visible bridge between the behavior catalog and the roadmap / proof state.

nodes.title: Review behavior catalog coherence
nodes.scope: A periodic-review flow that surfaces catalog coherence issues: implementation-shaped phrasing, duplicate / overlapping behaviors, missing-context behaviors, stale interfaces. Produces a list of recommended edits for the user to approve or reject.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: run coherence review; produce a list of issues (overlaps, drift, missing context). Falsification: review does not auto-edit the catalog without user approval.

playbook: Fixture a catalog with deliberate issues (overlap, implementation-shaped phrase, missing related links).
Run coherence review; verify the issue list is correct.
Approve / reject suggestions; verify the catalog updates only on approval.
nodes.rationale: Coherence review is the self-audit pass for the catalog — distinct from CRUD.

nodes.title: Maintain the system architecture record
nodes.scope: Architecture record CRUD under docs/architecture/. Subsystem records, technology choices, delivery / operations posture. Architecture-record edits are policy-gated (significant decisions need approval per M-0024).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: edit a subsystem record; change is recorded. Falsification: edit without permission rejected; significant changes require approval per policy.

playbook: Edit a subsystem record; verify the change.
Trigger a 'significant change' (e.g. swap a technology choice); verify the approval requirement is surfaced.
Negative walks: permission, illegal schema.
nodes.rationale: Architecture record maintenance is the architect-system flow — distinct from product brief (R-0051) and roadmap (R-0057).

nodes.title: Review architecture tradeoffs
nodes.scope: A view that surfaces the tradeoffs encoded in the architecture: what was chosen, what was rejected, why. Supports comparing alternatives. Tradeoff records sit alongside architecture records and are linked.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: view tradeoffs for a subsystem; see chosen + rejected with rationale. Positive: link to behavior or roadmap impact. Falsification: rejected alternatives are not silently re-promoted by edits.

playbook: View tradeoffs for a subsystem; verify the chosen + rejected entries.
Compare a hypothetical alternative; verify the comparison surfaces.
Cross-link to a behavior; verify the linkage.
nodes.rationale: Tradeoff review is its own analytical surface — different from authoring the architecture record.

nodes.title: Record product decisions and assumptions
nodes.scope: Decision-memory CRUD: record decisions made during planning (with date, context, rationale) and assumptions in play (with confidence, source, expiry). Stored under docs/product/decisions/ or similar.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: record a decision with rationale; record an assumption with confidence and expiry. Positive: list decisions and assumptions per project. Falsification: decisions cannot be silently edited without producing a new revision.

playbook: Record a decision; record an assumption; verify both appear.
Edit a decision; verify the prior revision is preserved.
Filter by date range; verify time-bounded queries work.
nodes.rationale: Decision and assumption recording is the durable memory the rest of the planning loop depends on.

nodes.title: Preserve rejected alternatives
nodes.scope: When a decision is made, also record the alternatives that were considered and rejected, with reasons. Surfaced alongside decisions in views; supports re-evaluation when context changes (R-0067).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: record a decision with rejected alternatives; alternatives appear with rejection rationale. Falsification: rejected alternatives cannot be silently elevated to chosen without new decision record.

playbook: Record a decision with 2-3 rejected alternatives; verify they appear.
Re-promote one alternative via a new decision; verify the prior decision is preserved with its history.
Negative walk: silent elevation rejected.
nodes.rationale: Rejected alternatives are first-class memory — different proof from decision recording (no-silent-promotion invariant).

nodes.title: Reuse prior user preferences when shaping work
nodes.scope: When shaping a new spec / decision / planning artifact, surface the user's prior preferences (e.g. preferred conventions, past style choices) so they don't have to re-state them. User can confirm, override, or update preferences.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: shape a new spec; prior preferences are surfaced for confirmation. Positive: override applies to new work but preserves the prior preference history. Falsification: preferences are not silently applied without user confirmation.

playbook: Set a preference (e.g. naming convention); shape a new spec; verify the preference is offered.
Override; verify the new work uses the override; preference history is preserved.
Negative walk: preference applied without confirmation.
nodes.rationale: Preference reuse is the surface of decision memory during shaping — distinct from passive recording.

nodes.title: Surface stale assumptions when context changes
nodes.scope: Detect when an assumption's context has changed (e.g. assumption expired by date, contradicting evidence appeared, related decision changed) and surface the assumption for review.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: an assumption past its expiry surfaces as stale; an assumption contradicted by new context surfaces. Falsification: stale flag does not auto-invalidate the assumption — user reviews and decides.

playbook: Set an assumption with near-term expiry; advance time; verify the assumption surfaces as stale.
Add new context that contradicts an assumption; verify the assumption surfaces.
Resolve / re-confirm; verify resolution is recorded.
nodes.rationale: Stale assumption detection is the active half of decision memory — distinct from passive recording.

nodes.title: Propose a planning change without accepting it
nodes.scope: Submit a proposed change to behavior catalog / architecture / roadmap as a PROPOSAL (not yet accepted). Proposals carry rationale and impact analysis; they don't immediately change canon. Pairs with R-0069 (review).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: submit a proposal targeting a behavior or architecture record; proposal is queued for review. Falsification: proposal does not change canon until accepted; submitter cannot self-accept without permission.

playbook: Submit a proposal to add a behavior; verify it's queued not accepted.
Inspect canon; verify nothing changed.
Negative walks: self-accept without permission rejected.
nodes.rationale: Proposals are the first-class change-request surface for planning — separating propose from accept enables review (R-0069).

nodes.title: Review a proposed planning change
nodes.scope: Review a queued planning proposal: see the proposed change, its rationale, predicted impact, related affected behaviors / specs / roadmap nodes. Accept (apply to canon) or reject (preserve in history).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: reviewer sees proposal + impact; accept applies to canon; reject preserves proposal as historical record. Falsification: reviewer without permission cannot accept; rejected proposals are not silently re-applied.

playbook: With a queued proposal, review it; verify impact analysis is shown.
Accept; verify canon updates and proposal is marked accepted.
Reject another proposal; verify it's preserved as historical with reason.
nodes.rationale: Review is the governance counterpart to propose. Two specs because the actor and proof concerns differ.

nodes.title: Resolve conflicting product direction
nodes.scope: When two accepted product positions conflict (e.g. mission vs success signal, two behavior outcomes contradicting), surface the conflict and run a resolution flow. Resolution is recorded as a decision (R-0064).

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: a fixtured conflict is surfaced; resolution flow records the chosen direction and the rationale. Falsification: conflict resolution does not silently delete one side — both positions remain in history.

playbook: Fixture a conflicting pair (e.g. mission says X, success signal implies not-X).
Run resolution; choose a direction; verify the decision is recorded with rationale and both prior positions preserved.
Negative walk: silent deletion rejected.
nodes.rationale: Conflict resolution is its own meta-behavior — depends on review (R-0069) infrastructure but has distinct outcomes.

nodes.title: Assess implementation against accepted behaviors
nodes.scope: Run an assessment pass that classifies each accepted behavior against current implementation: missing, implemented (code present, no proof), asserted (active BDD), stale (proof exists but seems out of date), regressed (proof was passing, now failing), or uncertain (insufficient signal). Assessment is read-only; it produces recommendations, not state changes.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: run assessment; receive a per-behavior classification with rationale. Falsification: assessment never silently changes verification_status — recommendations only.

playbook: Fixture a project with mixed states (some implemented, some asserted, some unimplemented).
Run assessment; verify the classification matches the fixtures.
Inspect; verify the verification_status field on behaviors did NOT auto-change.
Negative walk: confirm read-only invariant.
nodes.rationale: Assessment as a read-only analytical pass keeps the responsibility of applying status changes with the catalog-maintenance node (R-0059).

nodes.title: Review implementation assessment uncertainty
nodes.scope: Surface the 'uncertain' classifications from R-0071 with the specific signals that caused the uncertainty (e.g. 'proof exists but the test was last run 90 days ago', 'code references a behavior but no obvious entry point'). Lets the user decide whether to investigate or accept the uncertainty.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: view uncertain assessments with the underlying signals; can mark as investigate / accept-uncertain. Falsification: marking as accepted-uncertain is recorded with rationale; cannot silently downgrade to 'implemented'.

playbook: After R-0071, view the uncertain bucket; verify each entry shows its underlying signals.
Mark one as 'investigate'; verify it queues a follow-up.
Mark another as 'accept uncertain'; verify the rationale is recorded.
nodes.rationale: Uncertainty review is the human-in-the-loop side of assessment — distinct from the read-only classification pass.

nodes.title: Create a draft spec manually
nodes.scope: Manual spec creation: user authors a spec from scratch with title, intent, and initial scope. Spec starts in Draft state and is owned by the active project.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: create a draft spec; spec exists in Draft. Falsification: spec without title rejected; cross-project visibility blocked.

playbook: Per interface, create a spec; verify Draft state; verify project scoping.
Negative walks: missing title; cross-project.
nodes.rationale: Manual creation is the entry-point — every other creation path (R-0074, R-0075) follows similar mechanics with prefilled content.

nodes.title: Prefill a draft spec from an external ticket
nodes.scope: Pull an external ticket (Linear, Jira, GitHub Issue) and prefill a draft spec with its title, body, and metadata. Tanren is system-of-record for the spec from that point on; external ticket is referenced but not mirrored.

expected_evidence.description: Per interface — Positive: prefill a spec from a fixtured ticket; spec is Draft, references the ticket. Falsification: prefill does not mirror further ticket changes; cross-tracker isolation.

playbook: Connect a fixtured tracker (or fixture connection); pull a ticket; verify the spec is created Draft with prefilled content.
Edit the ticket externally; verify the spec is unchanged.
Negative walks: invalid ticket ID; cross-tracker.
nodes.rationale: Prefilling from external is structurally distinct from manual creation — different data source, different no-mirror invariant.

nodes.title: Turn roadmap items into specs
nodes.scope: From a roadmap node (whether the executable DAG or the human roadmap context), generate a draft spec prefilled with the node's behaviors, dependencies, and rationale. Multiple roadmap items can become a single spec; one roadmap item can become multiple specs.
